Sunday, May 16, 2021 

12:16 PM 

Fairy Strength 

Prerequisite: Fairy 

 

Fairy warriors often have to contend with foes much larger than them. Normally their tiny bodies don't stand a chance, but a bit of magical strength will easily level the playing field. And few creatures can stand the shame of being knocked out cold by someone not even a fraction of their weight.  

•Your Strength score increases by 1, to a maximum of 20. 

•At a negligible cost to your vitality you may use your bonus action to magically increase your strength until you lose concentration. You make yourself count as small in regards to wielding weapons, grappling or carrying objects. You can also use this increased strength to attack, although doing so ends the effect. As an action you strike a creature with magical might. Make an attack with a melee or thrown weapon or a melee spell attack. On a hit, the attack deals an additional 1d6 damage or 1d12 force damage if it's a spell attack.

Samstag, 25. März 2023 

15:24 

Friend of the Forest 

Prerequisite: Dryad 

 

By gaining a greater understanding of your connection to your spirit tree, you become more aware of the world around you, granting you the following benefits: 

•You gain a bonus to your initiative equal to your proficiency bonus. 

•While inside a forest you gain advantage on Wisdom skill checks. 

•You learn druidic, the language of the wild. You understand all sentient creatures and can communicate meaning with them, even if you don't share a language or the creature cannot speak. How complicated these ideas can be depends on the intelligence of those who participate in the conversation. A creature with 2 or less intelligence cannot be communicated with in any meaningful way. This form of communication works through a combination of speech, gestures, and magic. To communicate in this way, both participants must perceive each other with their own senses (druidic cannot be recorded or transmitted electronically). As long as the participants can see each other, druidic allows for silent communication.

Giant Toughness 

Prerequisite: Giant 

 

By specifically training your resilience you can turn your giant body into a living tank, granting you the following benefits:  

•Your hit point maximum increases by an amount equal to your level when you gain this feat. Whenever you gain a level thereafter, your hit point maximum increases by an additional 1 hit points. 

•When an attacker that you can see hits you with an attack, you can use your reaction to half the bludgeoning, piercing, and slashing damage you would take.

Human Prodigiousness 

Prerequisite: Human 

 

You train the already considerable endurance your human body, granting you the following benefits:  

•You gain advantage on death saving throws and failing a death saving throw does not cause you to fall unconscious. 

•Your vitality maximum increases by an amount equal to your level when you gain this feat. Whenever you gain a level thereafter, your vitality maximum increases by an additional 1 vitality.

Kobold Reflexes 

Prerequisite: Kobold 

 

You are especially nimble while in situations that threaten your life. You gain the following benefits: 

•Your movement does not provoke opportunity attacks. 

•If you have half your maximum hit point or less, your movement speed increases by half your walking speed. (both rounded down)

Squat Nimbleness 

Prerequisite: Small Size 

 

You are uncommonly nimble for your species, granting you the following benefits: 

•Your speed increases by 2 meters (5 feet). 

•You gain proficiency in the Dexterity (Acrobatics) or Strength (Athletics) skill (your choice). 

•You have advantage on any Strength (athletics) or Dexterity (acrobatics) check you make to escape from being grappled. 

•You can take the disengage action, and the dodge action as a bonus action, on each of your turns.

Chapter 6: Using Ability Scores- 

Monday, September 27, 2021 

4:29 PM 

Chapter 6: Using Ability Scores (toc) 

Six ability scores provide a quick description of every creature's physical and mental characteristics: 

•Strength, measuring physical power and speed 

•Dexterity, measuring reflexes and agility 

•Constitution, measuring endurance and hardiness 

•Intelligence, measuring logical reasoning and memory 

•Wisdom, measuring perception and improvisation 

•Charisma, measuring willpower and confidence 

Your ability scores summarize the physical capabilities of your body. If you were to compare your body to a computer, your soul would be the operating system and your ability scores are a spec-sheet detailing the capabilities of the hardware. A high strength score means that you are muscular and physically capable of stemming heavy weights. A low intelligence score means you have trouble remembering details and trying to understand complicated things simply feel difficult. Training can slowly enhance and neglect can cause these abilities to deteriorate. It needs to be mentioned, that the lower an ability score is, the easier it is to increase through training. The higher it is, the more training is needed to increase it further. 

You inherit these attributes from your patron, as they are all defined by the biological characteristics of your body. Though you retain your personality and memories, even your mental abilities are influenced by your physical form to no small degree. These abilities can be trained and honed through effort, represented by the periodic ability score increases or feats you receive as you survive the New World. 

A score of 10 is the normal human baseline. It represents a human who has put no, or only minimal effort into developing that skill, but also hasn't particularly neglected it. Some races are inherently better at certain abilities. For example, elves naturally have better reflexes and are more agile than humans, which is why they receive a bonus to Dexterity. A score of 14 or 15 is normal for those who use that ability often. Most soldiers or specialists from the other world will have 14 or 15 in two, or maybe even three ability scores. A score of 16 or 17 is normal for someone who specifically trains that ability. People like that are often mages. A score of 18 is the highest that a normal person can reach and requires focused and almost daily training. A score of 20 and beyond is reserved for the Olympic athletes and archmages of the world. Those who have dedicated their lives to training that specific aspect of their body, and have reached the pinnacle of mortal potential. 

Each ability also has a modifier, derived from the score and ranging from -5 (for an ability score of 1) to +10 (for a score of 30). The Ability Scores and Modifiers table notes the ability modifiers for the range of possible ability scores, from 1 to 30. 

  

Ability Scores and Modifiers Table

Proficiency Bonus (toc) 

You have a proficiency bonus determined by your level (see your class description). Your proficiency bonus represents that you have experience with doing a certain thing. A feature that allows you to add your proficiency bonus to a check (such as a skill proficiency) simply means that because you know how to do things of that type or have broad knowledge of the subject matter, your chance of success when trying to do something that you do not know exactly how to do, or where success is not guaranteed, is increased. 

Your proficiency bonus can’t be added to a single die roll or other number more than once. For example, if two different rules say you can add your proficiency bonus to a Wisdom saving throw, you nevertheless add the bonus only once when you make the save. 

Occasionally, your proficiency bonus might be multiplied or divided (doubled or halved, for example) before you apply it. Just like before, if multiple features would double it, you only apply one. If a feature would double your proficiency bonus in a skill you are not proficient in, you multiply by 0 and gain no benefit. 

  

  

Ability Checks (toc) 

An ability check tests your physical abilities in an effort to overcome a challenge. Whenever there is an event, other than an attack, where the outcome is not certain, the GM calls for an ability check. 

For every ability check, the GM decides which of the six abilities is relevant to the task at hand. Based on the likelihood of success and failure the GM determines a Difficulty Class (DC) that is unknown to you. The bonuses your abilities grant to these increase or decrease your chance of success and thus modify your roll. The Typical Difficulty Classes table shows the most common DCs. 

  

Typical Difficulty Classes 

Task Difficulty 

	
 

DC 




Very easy 

	

5 




Easy 

	

10 




Medium 

	

15 




Hard 

	

20 




Very hard 

	

25 




Nearly impossible 

	

30 




  

To make an ability check, roll a d20 and add the relevant ability modifier. As with other d20 rolls, apply bonuses and penalties, and compare the total to the DC. If the total equals or exceeds the DC, the ability check is a success and you overcome the challenge at hand. Otherwise, it’s a failure, which means you make no progress, or make progress but suffer a setback. 

  

Contests 

Sometimes your efforts are directly opposed to another's. This is a contest and can be anything from you and another creature in a tug of war to you trying to kick down a door another creature is holding shut. In this case a contest is called. The creatures participating in the contest make ability checks appropriate to their efforts. They apply all appropriate bonuses and penalties, but instead of comparing the total to a DC, they compare the totals of their two checks. The participant with the higher check total wins the contest.  

If the contest results in a tie, the situation remains the same as it was before the contest. Thus, one contestant might win the contest by default. For example, if you are trying to kick down a door and you tie with the foe trying to keep it shut, the door remains closed. 

  

Skills (toc) 

Each ability covers a broad range of capabilities, including skills that you can be proficient in. Every skill represents how good you are at overcoming a certain category of tasks. It represents a specific aspect of an ability score, and proficiency in a skill means that you have knowledge that makes it easier to accomplish tasks that fall into this category. For example, a Dexterity check might reflect a character’s attempt to pull off an acrobatic stunt, to pickpocket someone, or to stay hidden. Each of these aspects of Dexterity has an associated skill: Acrobatics, Sleight of Hand, and Stealth, respectively. So a character who has proficiency in the Stealth skill is particularly good at Dexterity checks related to sneaking and hiding. What skills you're proficient with is determined by what skills you had before the fog. In some cases your patron may also have been so skilled at a certain task that the ingrained knowledge they pass on to you provides you with proficiency in a skill. The skills related to each ability score are shown in the following list. Constitution has no skills associated with it. Every skill is described in greater detail later in this chapter. 

  

Strength 

Athletics  

  

Dexterity  

Acrobatics 

Sleight of Hand 

Stealth  

  

Intelligence 

Arcana 

History 

Investigation 

Mechanics 

Science 

Technology 

  

Wisdom 

Animal Handling 

Insight 

Medicine 

Perception 

Survival 

  

Charisma  

Deception  

Intimidation  

Performance 

Persuasion 

  

Sometimes, the GM might ask for an ability check using a specific skill, for example,  “Make a Wisdom (Perception) check.” At other times, you might ask the GM if proficiency in a particular skill applies to a check. In either case, you may add your proficiency bonus to ability checks that involve that skill. Without proficiency in the skill, make a normal ability check. For example, if you attempt to open a digital lock for a house, the GM might ask for an Intelligence (Technology) check. If you're proficient in Technology, your proficiency bonus is added to the Intelligence check. If you lack that proficiency, you may ask the GM to use another in this case. For example, instead of rewiring the lock, you could use finesse to disable the locking mechanism and might be able to use Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) to overcome the challenge. If you have no relevant proficiency, you make a raw Intelligence check. 

  

Passive Checks 

A passive check is a special kind of ability check that doesn’t involve any die rolls. Such a check may represent your general aptitude, such as when you are attempting to craft something over and over again, or can be used when the GM wants to secretly determine whether you succeed at something, such as whether you notice an ambush down the road.  

Here’s how to determine a character’s total for a passive check:  

  

10 + all modifiers that normally apply to the check  

  

If you would have advantage on the check, add 5. In case of disadvantage, subtract 5. The game refers to a passive check total as a score. For example, if you have a Wisdom ability score of 15 and proficiency in Perception, you have a passive Wisdom (Perception) score of 14. The rules on hiding in the “Dexterity” section below rely on passive checks, as do the exploration rules in chapter 8. 

  

Working Together 

Sometimes two or more people team up to attempt a task. The one who’s leading the effort, or the one with the highest ability modifier, can make an ability check with advantage, reflecting the help provided by the assistant. In combat, this requires the Help action (see chapter 9). A creature can only provide help if the task is one that it could attempt alone. Additionally, some tasks can only be completed alone. Driving a car is not made easier when two people hold the wheel. 

  

Group Checks 

When more than one person is trying to accomplish something as a group, the GM might ask for a group ability check. In such a situation, those who are skilled at a particular task help cover those who aren’t. To make a group ability check, everyone in the group makes the ability check. If at least half the group succeeds, the  whole group succeeds. Otherwise, the group fails. For example, when the group is trying to scale a cliff, those who are particularly experienced or skilled climbers may compensate the lack of skill of others by giving them tips, or making sure they don't fall. Group checks are common when you decide to work together as a team. 

  

Saving Throws (toc) 

A saving throw, also called a save, represents an attempt to resist an unwanted effect, such as a harmful spell, a trap, a poison, a disease, or a similar threat. You usually don't choose when to make a saving throw. You are forced to make one in order to resist an effect you normally wouldn't want to be affected by. In most cases, you can choose to fail a saving throw. However if resisting the effect is something your body does automatically, as with most Constitution saving throws, the GM may determine you cannot fail the throw on purpose. When you do attempt a saving throw, roll a d20 and add the appropriate ability modifier. For example, you use your Dexterity modifier for a Dexterity saving throw. A saving throw can be modified by a situational bonus or penalty and can be made with advantage, or disadvantage, as determined by the GM. Your class and species may provide you with proficiency in a type of saving throw, allowing you to add your proficiency bonus when you make the roll. The Difficulty Class for a saving throw is determined by the effect that causes it. For example, the DC for a saving throw allowed by a spell is determined by the caster’s spellcasting ability and proficiency bonus. The result of a successful or failed saving throw is also detailed in the effect that calls for the save. Usually, a successful save means that a creature suffers no, or at least mitigates some, of the effects harm. 

  

Using Your Abilities (toc) 

Every task that a character or monster might attempt in the game is covered by one of the six abilities. This section explains in more detail what those abilities mean and how they are used. 

  

Strength 

Strength measures your physical strength. You need it to perform tasks that require brawn and to resist effects that would physically move you. 

  

Athletics 

Your Strength (Athletics) skill represented how well you can overcome obstacles that can or must be faced with brute force. Being proficient in Athletics means you have advanced knowledge about sports. 

A Strength (Athletics) check may be called for when you: 

•Attempt to kick down a door. 

•Try to jump an unusually long distance. 

•Attempt to grapple a creature. 

  

The Lore of Force 

Force is strong and cumbersome. Summoning it requires motions that are made with strength behind them, which is why it is enhanced by high, and hampered by low, Strength. 

  

Strength Attack Rolls and Damage 

You add your Strength modifier to your attack roll and your damage roll when attacking with a melee weapon such as a sword, a battleaxe, or a javelin. You use melee weapons to make melee attacks in hand-to-hand combat, and some of them can be thrown to make a ranged attack, for which you may use either Strength or Dexterity. 

  

Lifting and Carrying 

Your Strength score determines the amount of weight you can bear. The following terms define what you can lift or carry. 

Encumberment. Your maximum encumberment depends on your size. Multiply your Strength score by the multiplier listed in the Encumberment Modifier table. This is the weight that you can carry constantly and without it inhibiting your maneuverability. To make full use of your carrying capacity you need to wear a backpack. Otherwise, you can only carry what you're holding in your hands. 

Push, Drag, or Lift. You can push, drag, or lift a weight in kilograms up to four-times your maximum encumbrance. While pushing or dragging weight in excess of your maximum encumbrance, your speed drops to 1 meter. 

  

Carry Weight Modifier 

Size 

	
 

Multiplier 




Tiny 

	

Strength x 1 




Small 

	

Strength x 2 




Medium 

	

Strength x 4 




Large 

	

Strength x 8 




  

Strength Saves 

A Strength saving throw represents the physical strength you can quickly bring to bare as you attempt to resist an effect that would slow you down, impair your actions by physically blocking your movements, or move you against your will. 

  

Dexterity 

Dexterity measures how quickly you react and how easy it is for you to perform complicated movements. You need it to perform actions that require precision and to dodge out of the way of attacks.  

  

Acrobatics 

Your Dexterity (Acrobatics) skill represents how good you are at overcoming challenges that test your agility. Being proficient in Acrobatics means that you have advanced knowledge of how to move your body. 

An acrobatics check may be called for when you: 

•Attempt to walk a tightrope or balance across a narrow log. 

•Climb up a cliff. 

•Try to make a summersault while jumping. 

  

  

Sleight of Hand 

Your Dexterity (Sleight of Hand) skill represents how good you are at performing tasks that require precise and quick movements to complete, such as picking a pocket, disarming a mine, untying a knot, or hiding something in your pocket. 

Being proficient in Sleight of Hand means you have trained to manipulate objects with extreme precision and care. 

A Sleight of hand check may be called for when you: 

•Try to pickpocket someone. 

•Want to draw something accurately. 

  

Stealth 

Your Dexterity (Stealth) skill represents how good you are at concealing yourself, like when you are trying to hide, or when trying to avoid leaving tracks. 

Being proficient in stealth means you have advanced knowledge on how to conceal yourself. 

  

*Hiding* 

As long as you are not being revealed by an effect (see the Revealed condition) and a creature is unaware of your exact location (for example because you are not yet close enough to be spotted or it has lost sight of you), you may attempt to hide from it. Entering cover, being invisible, or breaking direct line of sight generally causes a creature to be unaware of your exact location and gives you the opportunity to hide. Your size affects how easy it is for you to break line of sight. A tiny creature can hide behind a large branch, while an entire car might not be enough to obscure a large one. 

You take the Hide action and make a Dexterity (Stealth) check against its Passive Perception. On a success, the creature doesn't notice your presence or loses track of you if it noticed you before. Outside of combat hiding requires you to move at a slow pace, meaning at half your speed. See the rules for traveling in Chapter 7 for details. 

Passive Perception. A creature's Passive Perception is its passive Wisdom (Perception) score, which equals 10 + the creature's Wisdom modifier, as well as any other bonuses or penalties applied to that skill. If the creature has advantage, add 5. For disadvantage, subtract 5. For example, if a 1st-level character (with a proficiency bonus of +2) has a Wisdom of 15 (a +2 modifier) and proficiency in Perception, they have a passive Wisdom (Perception) of 14. 

Being noticed. Regardless of your stealth role, a creature that can see you will spot and reveal you. While you are hidden, you may enter a creature's line of sight without it spotting you. As a rule of thumb, as long as you are out of sight when you hide and end your turn out of sight, you remain hidden. In these cases, it is assumed that you wait for the creature to turn away from you before moving. 

If a creature has been alerted to your presence, it may attempt to search for you. In this case, it makes a Wisdom (Perception) check against your passive Stealth score. On a success, it determines your location, even if it can't see you. A creature can attempt to find you even when it's unable to see you, such as when you are invisible or heavily obscured. On a success, it learns your approximate location and can track you until you hide from it again. 

Attacking a creature, dashing, or taking any other action reveals you to all nearby creatures unless that action is a silent action. 

Silent actions. Silent actions are activities that make no or only very little noise and cause no or only very little light to be emitted. An action is only silent if its description states that it is. Your GM may allow you to attempt an action that would normally not be silent silently using a Dexterity (Slight of Hand) check. Actions that cause a lot of noise or create light, such as non-silent spells and firearms, cannot be attempted silently. 

  

The Lore of Light 

Light is fast and precise. Summoning and controlling it requires complicated and precise movements, which is why it is enhanced by high, and hampered by low, Dexterity. 

  

Dexterity Attack Rolls and Damage 

You add your Dexterity modifier to your attack roll and your damage roll when attacking with a ranged weapon, such as a bow. You also add your Dexterity modifier to your attack roll, but not your damage roll when attacking with a firearm. You may add your Dexterity modifier to your attack roll and your damage roll when attacking with a melee weapon that has the finesse property, such as a dagger or a shortsword. As a tiny creature, you may wield any tiny weapon with Dexterity. 

  

Armor Class 

Depending on the armor you wear, you might add some or all of your Dexterity modifier to your Armor Class. Your Armor Class (AC) represents how well-protected you are from attacks. The higher it is, the harder you are to hit. Things that contribute to your AC include your size, the armor you wear, shields you carry, and your Dexterity modifier. 

Your Armor Class = Your species' Base Armor Class + Your Dexterity modifier 

Your species’ base armor class is determined by your size. A very small creature, like a fairy or a cat, is inherently harder to hit than a large creature, like a giant. How high your base armor class is detailed in your species.  

Wearing armor makes it more likely that a hit will simply glance off without doing damage. A high Dexterity score means that you'll have an easier time dodging out of an attack's way, while a low one means enemies have an easier time tracking you. No matter how large you are and how low your Dexterity is, your base armor class cannot be lower than 1. 

How armor modifies your Armor Class is described in detail in the chapter on armor. Usually, it provides a flat bonus but may limit the amount you may add from your Dexterity modifier. You need to have a certain amount of Strength to wear and use armor and shields effectively. There are drawbacks to wearing armor or carrying a shield if you lack the required Strength, as explained in the section on armor in Chapter 4: Equipment. 

Some class features and special abilities give you a different way to calculate your AC. If you have multiple features that give you different ways to calculate your AC, you choose which one to use. 

  

Initiative 

At the beginning of combat, you roll initiative by making a Dexterity check. Initiative determines the order of creatures' turns in combat, starting with the highest and ending with the lowest. 

  

Dexterity Saves 

A Dexterity saving throw represents the speed and finesse you bring to bear in order to react to something quickly. For example, an explosion will obliterate anything it catches, but by predicting where it will go off you can at the very least get out of the epicenter and mitigate the damage it deals. Some effects, like a bolt of plasma, or an incoming stampede can only be avoided by dodging out of harm's way. 

  

Constitution 

Constitution measures how well your body can resist exertion and trauma. You need it to resist damage, poisons, and diseases. 

  

Constitution Checks 

Constitution checks are uncommon, and no skills apply to Constitution checks, because the endurance this ability represents is largely passive rather than involving a conscious effort. However, a Constitution check can model your attempt to push beyond normal limits. 

A Constitution check may be called for when you: 

•Hold your breath 

•March or labor for hours without rest 

•Go without sleep 

•Survive without food or water 

•Drain an entire mug of beer in one go 

  

The Lore of Frost 

Frost is slow and steady. Summoning and controlling it is a taxing endeavor, which is why it is enhanced by high, and hampered by low, Constitution. 

  

Hit Points (Health) 

Your Constitution modifier contributes to your hit points. Whenever you increase your maximum hit points by leveling, you add your Constitution modifier to the increase. 

If your Constitution modifier changes, your hit point maximum changes as well, as though you had the new modifier from 0th level.  

  

Constitution Saves 

A Constitution saving throw represents how well your body can handle being pushed to its limits. For example, toxins and poisons cause much less harm in a body that can combat their effect quickly with a powerful immune system. A strong heart makes it easier not to be overwhelmed by electricity coursing through you. Magical effects that want to interfere with your bodily functions are also less likely to affect someone whose body is in perfect condition. 

  

Intelligence 

Intelligence measures how well you can remember information and solve logic problems. You need it to investigate mysteries and identify things. 

  

Arcana 

Your Intelligence (Arcana) skill represents how good you are at identifying and working with spells and magic items. 

You can only obtain proficiency in the arcana skill through your class or a feat. 

A Wisdom (Arcana) check may be called for when you: 

•Attempt to identify a magic item 

•Want to figure out what spell an opponent is casting 

•Try to identify a creature that is an aberration, a construct, or an elemental 

  

Investigation 

Your Intelligence (Investigation) skill represents how good you are at finding things, such as when scavenging or taking your time to search a room. Whereas Perception is the skill used when you need to notice something quickly and glance across the room, Investigation is used when you take your time to search every nook and cranny. Being proficient in this skill means that you know how to thoroughly search for something, be that a hidden object or information. 

An Investigation check may be called for when you: 

•Search an abandoned house for loot. 

•Take your time to look for clues. 

  

Scavenging. As you wander the New World, you will invariably come across different locations that are either abandoned or forcibly cleared of their inhabitants. If you want to search a location for valuables, you must take a few hours to search it. After searching it, you make an Intelligence (Investigation) check. The GM determines what you find depending on the result of your check. 

  

Mechanics 

Your Intelligence (Mechanics) skill represents how good you are at understanding, constructing, and repairing mechanical components. Being proficient in mechanics means you have advanced knowledge of physics and engineering. 

A mechanics check may be called for when you: 

•Attempt to repair a broken vehicle. 

•Try to jerry rig a piece of equipment. 

•Craft annitem. 

•Want to determine the function of a machine. 

  

Nature 

Your Intelligence (Nature) skill represents how good you are at identifying natural creatures or natural phenomena. Being proficient in nature means you have detailed knowledge of biology or natural lore. 

A nature check may be called for when you: 

•Attempt to identify a creature that is a beast, fey, monstrosity, ooze, or plant. 

  

Religion 

Your Intelligence (Religion) skill represents how good you are at identifying phenomena caused by divine beings or the Void. Being proficient in religion means you have advanced knowledge of things from beyond the veil. 

A religion check may be called for when you: 

•Want to know the nature of a divine sanctuary 

•Attempt to identify a creature that is a celestial, fiend, or undead 

  

Science 

Your Intelligence (Science) skill represents how much you know about chemistry and physics. Being proficient in science means that you have advanced knowledge of these subjects and know how to apply that knowledge. 

A science check may be called for when you: 

•Attempt to identify a chemical or other compound. 

•Want to improvise an explosive. 

•Identify the effects of a spell or metaphysical phenomenon. 

  

Technology 

Your Intelligence (Technology) skill represents how good you are at working with electronic devices, like fixing or hacking a computer, or setting up a communication array. Being proficient in this skill means you know how electronics work and how they can be manipulated. 

You can only gain proficiency in this skill through your background or a feat. 

A technology check may be called for when you: 

•Try to hack a computer. 

•Want to repair an electronic device. 

•Attempt to determine the function of a piece of modern technology. 

  

Intelligence Saves 

An Intelligence saving throw is called for when an effect requires logic and reason to resist. Spells that distort your thoughts, usually to debilitate or deal psychic damage to you, have a harder time affecting the logical and well-structured minds of intelligent people. 

  

The Lore of Lightning 

Lightning is predictable. The paths it takes and the patterns it creates are always the same, but numerous and complicated. Summoning and controlling electrical energy require logic and knowledge and it is thus enhanced by high, and hampered by low, intelligence. 

  

Wisdom 

Wisdom measures your intuition, or how good you are at making split-second decisions and how much attention you pay to the world around you. You need it to spot things quickly and to resist effects that would trick you. 

  

Animal Handling 

Your Wisdom (Animal Handling) skill represents how good you are at dealing with animals. 

Being proficient in Animal Handling means that you have advanced knowledge of the behavior of animals and have trained to work with their unpredictable nature. 

A Wisdom (Animal Handling) check may be called for when you: 

•Try to calm down a wild animal. 

•Attempt to convince an animal to help you. 

•Want to predict what a beast will do.  

  

Taming. Humans, pets, and livestock make up over 90 percent of mammals, meaning that most monsters used to be cows, pigs, dogs, cats, or other domesticated animals. They were used to human contact in their previous lives and even though their new instincts make them a lot more feral, many are willing to return to serving humanoids, for a price. After pacifying a creature, either by wounding or talking it down, you may attempt to tame it by succeeding a Wisdom (Animal Handling) check. If an animal can be tamed, the DC of this check is recorded in its stat block and told to you by your GM. After recruiting the animal you must feed it daily or it will abandon you. The amount of rations you need to pay also depends on the creature's statistics and is told to you by your GM. This daily upkeep is deducted at the end of each night’s rest. If it is not paid, the animal companion does not gain the benefits of a long rest and instead gains 1 level of exhaustion that costs a number of extra rations equal to their daily upkeep to remove, in addition to their normal upkeep. 

  

Insight 

Your Wisdom (Insight) skill represents how good you are at determining someone's, emotions, motives, and wishes. 

Being proficient in this skill means that you have advanced knowledge of the behavior and instincts of humanoids. 

A Wisdom (Insight) check may be called for when you: 

•Try to tell if someone's lying 

•Want to discern an intelligent creature's motives 

  

  

Medicine 

Your Wisdom (Medicine) skill represents how good you are at handling medical emergencies. Being proficient in this skill means you know how to use medical equipment and have at least some knowledge of anatomy. 

A medicine check may be called for when you: 

•Stabilize a creature. 

•Attempt to identify or cure a disease. 

•Try to perform surgery. 

  

Perception 

Wisdom (Perception) check lets you spot, hear, or otherwise detect the presence of something. It measures your general awareness of your surroundings and the keenness of your senses. Being proficient in this skill means that your senses are particularly honed. 

A perception check may be called for when you: 

•Try to spot a detail in passing. 

•Need to spot a trap you're about to walk into. 

•Want to know the general location of an invisible or obscured creature. 

  

*Searching*  

When you search for a hidden object or creature by using an action to quickly look over an area, the GM typically asks you to make a Wisdom (Perception) check. Such a check can be used to find hidden details or other information and clues that are not immediately obvious, to spot a hidden foe, or to identify the approximate location of an invisible or obscured creature. 

  

Survival 

Your Wisdom (Survival) skill represents how good you are at surviving in the wilderness and how much you know about nature. Being proficient in this skill means you have advanced knowledge of how to survive in the wilderness and the natural world. 

A survival check may be called for when you: 

•Try to forage for food. 

•Want to track a creature. 

  

*Foraging and Hunting* 

You may spend a day stalking through the landscape foraging, or hunting. When you do, you make a Wisdom (Survival) check. Depending on your result, the terrain you are in, and what time of year it is, the GM will tell you what you find and may have you run into an appropriate random encounter. 

  

Wisdom Saves 

A Wisdom saving throw is called for when an effect requires quick wits and attentiveness to resist. Spells that attempt to warp your perception of reality and can be resisted by noticing the manipulation allow for wisdom saving throws. 

  

The Lore of Fire 

Fire is unpredictable. It flares and fizzles and consumes anything it can touch. Summoning and controlling heat requires the ability to move with the flame. It cannot be contained or commanded, only guided, and is thus enhanced by high, and hampered by low, Wisdom. 

  

Charisma 

Charisma measures the strength of your personality, willpower, and confidence. You need it to resist things that try to forcefully replace or completely subsume your will. 

  

Charisma Checks 

A Charisma check might arise when you try to influence or entertain others, when you try to make an impression or tell a convincing lie, or when you are navigating a tricky social situation. 

  

Deception 

Your Charisma (Deception) skill determines how good you are at hiding the truth, either verbally or through your actions. Proficiency in this skill means you are particularly capable when it comes to deceiving others. 

A Deception check may be called for when you: 

•Tell a lie. 

•Try to hide your true motifs. 

  

Intimidation 

Your Charisma (Intimidation) skill represents how good you are at influencing someone through overt threats, hostile actions, and physical violence. Being proficient in this skill means you are particularly good at making others feel help- or powerless. 

An Intimidation check may be called for when you: 

•Threaten a prisoner. 

•Try to make someone back off from a fight. 

  

Performance 

Your Charisma (Performance) skill represents how good you are at influencing a crowd. Being proficient in this skill means you know how to stand in front of a crowd. 

A Performance check may be called for when you: 

•Hold an inspiring speech. 

•DJ a party. 

  

Persuasion 

Your Charisma (Persuasion) skill represents how good you are at influencing others with reason, arguments, or flattery. Being proficient in this skill means that you are particularly apt at debating a point. 

A Persuasion check may be called for when you: 

•Want to convince a rival group that peace is in everyone's best interest. 

•Haggle for a better price with a trader. 

  

Charisma Saves 

A Charisma saving throw is called for when willpower and mental presence are needed. Spells that attempt to subsume your personality or force you to act in a specific way can usually only be resisted through force of will and thus call for Charisma saving throws. 

  

The Lore of Life 

Life is difficult to influence. Those that wish to manipulate it must force the needed energy into the world. Because of its resisting nature, life magic is thus enhanced by high, and hampered by low, Charisma. 

  

  

Conflicting change.

Chapter 7: Basic Survival 

Thursday, December 30, 2021 

6:18 PM 

Chapter 7: Basic Survival 

  

Stalking through the corrupted ruins of a city. Running for your life from an enraged owlbear. Facing down a rival group as they seek to snatch a cache of Old World guns from under your nose. As you navigate through the harsh and unforgiving landscape of the New World, you will be constantly tested, facing challenges that will push you to the limit. You must be resourceful and quick-thinking, gather supplies, build a community, and protect each other from the dangers that lurk all around. Yet, despite the seemingly insurmountable odds, there is hope. With each step you take, with each challenge you overcome, you are building a foundation upon which future generations can thrive. 

This chapter covers the basics of the rules that govern your survival, from the mechanics of movement to the complexities of social interaction and building a settlement. The rules for resting are also in this section, along with a discussion of the activities your character might pursue between missions and when taking their long rests. 

Whether survivors are exploring the ruins of a city or mediating between two settlements on the brink of war, the story follows a natural rhythm, as outlined in this book’s introduction: 

1.The GM describes the environment. 

2.The players describe what they want to do. 

3.The GM narrates the results of their actions. 

Typically, the GM uses a map as an outline of the adventure, tracking the survivors’ progress as they explore buildings or wilderness regions. Since an SoaNW campaign usually plays in a real world location, the GM will most likely use a real map of that location, noting down points of interest on it as players discover them. This is called a World Map and in most cases a World Map uses a hexagonal grid. 

  

Time 

Time is an essential part of a Saga of a New World campaign. It determines the weather, the progress of other survivors and is both your worst enemy and your best friend. Every campaign begins on the morning of October 3rd (between 9-12 AM CET depending on when you wake up). The first and biggest challenge is acquiring shelter and a reliable food supply, because your time is limited. Winter is on the horizon. Those caught unprepared will quickly die from starvation, if the cold doesn't get them first during these darkest days of the year. Even after that, many mechanics the GM uses depend on how much time has passed as factions set plans into motion to secure their dominance of your region. 

When players attempt a task, the GM determines the time it requires. The GM might use a different time scale depending on the context of the situation at hand. While exploring, your movement happens on a scale of minutes. It takes you about a minute to creep down a short street to a house, a few minutes to pick the lock, and a good ten minutes to search the house itself for enemies. 

When performing tasks like turning a building upside down searching for loot, or traveling a short distance of a few kilometers, hours are a more appropriate timeframe. 

For long journeys, a scale of days works best. In combat and other fast-paced situations, the game relies on 6-second intervals called rounds.

The Environment 

Saturday, January 1, 2022 

10:42 AM 

The Environment 

By its nature, surviving involves delving into places that are dark, dangerous, and full of mysteries to be explored. The rules in this section cover some of the most important ways in which you will interact with the environment. 

  

Falling 

A fall from a great height is one of the most common hazards you may face. A single slip is enough to send you tumbling to your probable doom. When a creature begins a fall no longer than 150 meters, it can choose to hit the ground immediately, or at the end of its turn. If the fall is greater than 150 meters, the creature descends at a rate of 150 meters per turn. A creature takes 3 (1d6) bludgeoning damage for every 3 meters (10 feet) it fell, to a maximum of 70 (20d6) bludgeoning damage. A falling creature must succeed a Dexterity saving throw or land prone (see the condition). The DC of this saving throw equals the damage it takes from the fall. 

  

Suffocating 

A creature can hold its breath for a number of minutes equal to its Constitution modifier (minimum of 30 seconds, or 5 rounds). 

When a creature runs out of breath or is choking, it can survive for a number of rounds equal to its Constitution modifier (minimum of 1 round). At the start of its next turn, it drops to 0 hit points, falls unconscious, and is downed (see the condition). It can't regain hit points, automatically fails death saving throws, and can't be stabilized until it can breathe again. A creature that dropped to 0 hit points as a result of being suffocated immediately stabilizes and wakes up from being unconscious when it gains the ability to breathe, however, it remains downed. 

For example, Janina has a Constitution of 14 can thus hold her breath for 2 minutes. If she starts suffocating, she has 2 rounds to reach air before she drops to 0 hit points. 

  

Interacting with Objects 

Most interactions with objects are easily resolved. You tell your GM what you want to do and they, if further elaboration is required, narrate what happens . 

You can also damage objects with weapons and spells. Objects are immune to poison and psychic damage, but otherwise, they can be affected by physical and magical attacks much like creatures can. 

The GM determines an object's Armor Class and hit points depending on its resilience, size, and the material it is made of, and might decide that certain objects have resistance or immunity to certain kinds of attacks. (It's hard to cut a rope with a baseball club, for example.) Objects can't dodge your attacks and for large objects, its AC usually represents how hard that object is to damage with a hit. Objects always fail saving throws. 

A survivor can also attempt a Strength (Athletics) check to break an object. The DM sets the DC for any such check. 

 

  

Looting 

In the harsh and unforgiving New World, one of the most rewarding and satisfying aspects is the act of looting. As you vanquish your foes and emerge victorious from battle, the spoils of war lie before you, waiting to be claimed. You search the pockets of fallen enemies, retrieving any valuable items or equipment that may aid you in your journey. You carefully cut open the animals you have slain, salvaging the succulent meat within and utilizing their hides to craft sturdy boots or armor that will provide protection and warmth in the harsh wilderness.  

As already implied, there are two ways of looting: harvesting and searching. Looting usually requires no more than a few minutes as you quickly survey the area. If you want to spend a significant amount of time taking everything of value in an area and moving it to your settlement, see Scavenging in chapter 8: Settlements.

At the start of its next turn, it drops to 0 hit points, falls unconscious, and is downed (see the condition). It can't regain hit points, automatically fails death saving throws, and can't be stabilized until it can breathe again. A creature that dropped to 0 hit points as a result of being suffocated immediately stabilizes and wakes up from being unconscious when it gains the ability to breathe, however, it remains downed. 

For example, Janina has a Constitution of 14 can thus hold her breath for 2 minutes. If she starts suffocating, she has 2 rounds to reach air before she drops to 0 hit points. 

  

Interacting with Objects 

Most interactions with objects are easily resolved. You tell your GM what you want to do and they, if further elaboration is required, narrate what happens . 

You can also damage objects with weapons and spells. Objects are immune to poison and psychic damage, but otherwise, they can be affected by physical and magical attacks much like creatures can. 

The GM determines an object's Armor Class and hit points depending on its resilience, size, and the material it is made of, and might decide that certain objects have resistance or immunity to certain kinds of attacks. (It's hard to cut a rope with a baseball club, for example.) Objects can't dodge your attacks and for large objects, its AC usually represents how hard that object is to damage with a hit. Objects always fail saving throws. 

A survivor can also attempt a Strength (Athletics) check to break an object. The DM sets the DC for any such check. 

 

  

Looting 

In the harsh and unforgiving New World, one of the most rewarding and satisfying aspects is the act of looting. As you vanquish your foes and emerge victorious from battle, the spoils of war lie before you, waiting to be claimed. You search the pockets of fallen enemies, retrieving any valuable items or equipment that may aid you in your journey. You carefully cut open the animals you have slain, salvaging the succulent meat within and utilizing their hides to craft sturdy boots or armor that will provide protection and warmth in the harsh wilderness.  

As already implied, there are two ways of looting: harvesting and searching. Looting usually requires no more than a few minutes as you quickly survey the area. If you want to spend a significant amount of time taking everything of value in an area and moving it to your settlement, see Scavenging in chapter 8: Settlements. 

Harvesting a creature means you cut it apart and convert its body into scrap and rations for later consumption. The amount of rations and scrap a creature yields is determined by your GM and is detailed in the creature’s stat block. Harvesting a creature outside of a settlement requires a Hunter’s Pack (see the tool kit). 

Searching an area or creature allows you to quickly gather whatever is lying around. This usually includes any weapons your enemies were wielding and whatever they have in their pockets. Your GM also determines what can be found. If you are only giving a location a quick search, your GM may ask for a Wisdom (Perception) check to determine if you find anything. 

Conflicting change.

Vision and Light 

Saturday, January 1, 2022 

10:44 AM 

Vision and Light 

The most fundamental tasks of adventuring - noticing danger, finding hidden objects, hitting an enemy in combat, and targeting a spell, to name just a few, rely heavily on your ability to perceive others. Lack of light and other effects that obscure vision can prove a significant hindrance. 

Obscurement 

A given area might be lightly or heavily obscured. In a lightly obscured area, such as dim light, patchy fog, or moderate foliage, creatures have disadvantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks that rely on sight. 

A heavily obscured area - such as absolute darkness, darkness, opaque fog, or dense foliage - blocks vision entirely. A creature effectively suffers from the blinded condition when trying to see something in that area. 

Illumination 

The presence or absence of light in an environment creates four categories of illumination: bright light, dim light, darkness, and absolute darkness. 

Bright light lets most creatures see normally. Even gloomy days provide bright light, as do flashlights, fires, and other sources of illumination within a specific radius. 

Dim light, also called shadows, creates a lightly obscured area. An area of dim light is usually a boundary between a source of bright light, such as a flashlight, and surrounding darkness. The soft light of twilight and dawn also counts as dim light. A particularly brilliant full moon might bathe the land in dim light. 

Darkness creates a heavily obscured area. Characters face darkness outdoors at night (even most moonlit nights), or within the confines of an unlit basement. 

Absolute Darkness creates a heavily obscured area and blinds creatures with darkvision. Characters face absolute darkness in unlit bunkers or caves deep underground. 

  

Special Senses 

Some creatures have special senses, like blindsight, darkvision, infravision, tremorsense, or truesight that only have a specific range. If you have a special sense, the listed range tells you at what range your special sense works perfectly. Unless the feature that grants you the special sense says otherwise, you can still track something as long as it's no more than twice as far away as the listed range. However, anything outside the original radius is lightly obscured. Anything more than twice as far away as the listed range is always heavily obscured to your special sense. 

  

Blindsight 

A creature with blindsight can perceive its surroundings without relying on sight, within the listed radius. Creatures without eyes, and creatures with echolocation or heightened senses, such as bats, have this sense. 

Darkvision 

Creatures with darkvision have exceptionally sensitive eyes that require only trace amounts of light to see. Within a specified range, a creature with darkvision can see in dim light as if it were bright light and darkness as if it were dim light, so areas of darkness are only lightly obscured as far as that creature is concerned. However, the creature can't discern color in darkness, only shades of gray. Darkvision provides no benefits in absolute darkness. 

Infravision 

A creature with infravision can see the infrared radiation emitted by warm objects and sees the world in temperature differentials, usually marked by shades of one or several colors. A creature with infravision can see even in absolute darkness and camouflage is useless against them. Infravision also penetrates smoke and fog, but not glass. 

Tremorsense 

A creature with tremorsense senses vibrations in the ground out to the listed range. Any creature that touches the ground in that area is revealed to the creature and it is not fooled by illusions that have no physical mass. 

Truesight 

A creature with truesight can, out to a specific range, see in normal and magical absolute darkness, see invisible creatures and objects, automatically detect visual illusions and succeed on saving throws against them, and perceives the original form of a shapechanger or a creature that is transformed by magic. Furthermore, the creature can see into the Void. 

Conflicting change.

Resting 

Saturday, January 1, 2022 

10:49 AM 

Resting 

Everyone needs to rest to recuperate their strength. It is the time when you are the most vulnerable and extremely time-consuming.  

 

Short Rest 

A short rest is a period of at least one hour spent outside of combat resting and tending to wounds.  

At the end of a short rest, you may spend one hit die. Roll the die and add your Constitution modifier to the result. You regain a number of hit points equal to the total. You may perform several short rests consecutively. Whenever your number of available hit dice reaches zero, you gain a level of exhaustion and the number of available hit dice resets to your maximum. 

  

Night's Rest 

A night's rest is a period of time where you sleep for as long as your species needs to sleep in a day. Combat, traveling, or performing any strenuous activity for more than a few minutes interrupts the night's rest, forcing you to start over. 

At the end of a night's rest, you expend a number of rations equal to the number of rations you need to consume each day, or increase your hunger by this amount if you are unable or unwilling to expend these rations. The number of rations you must consume each day is determined by your species. You then recover any missing vitality and may expend hit dice to restore hit points. Roll the die and add your Constitution modifier to the result. You regain a number of hit points equal to the total. You may decide to expend an additional hit die after each roll. As always, when the number of available hit dice reaches zero, you recover all of your hit dice and gain a level of exhaustion.  

  

Long Rest 

A long rest is a period of seven days of downtime you use to recover your strength. Taking damage, or a day of strenuous activity, like back-breaking labor, forced march, or anything that causes you to gain a level of exhaustion cancels the long rest and forces you to start over. 

At the end of a day that is to count for the long rest, expend a number of rations equal to the number of rations you need to consume each day, or increase your hunger by this amount if you are unable or unwilling to expend these rations. 

At the end of the long rest, you regain all your hit points, hit dice, and vitality and are cleansed of all exhaustion stacks. 

  

Downtime 

During a long rest, you will have a lot of time that you need to spend. You may simply assume that you spend that time reading, practicing using your abilities, or playing games and essentially skip past that time. You may also use that time to interact with NPCs. If you are at the head of a settlement you will most likely spend as much time as you can managing that settlement. Another option is to simply assume control of an allied NPC to play with during that time.

Tuesday, 26 December 2023 

22:37 

Foraging and Hunting 

If your rations are running low, you might want to forage and/or hunt. If you do, your GM will ask you to make one or more Wisdom (Survival) checks to determine what you find. The Game Master's Guide provides rules for how these checks, and their results, are determined. 

Strength in numbers holds true while wandering the woods as well. However, a larger group will rarely find more than a single individual and the more people you bring, the less likely you are to find and surprise prey.

Chapter 9: Combat 

Thursday, December 30, 2021 

6:29 PM 

Chapter 9: Combat 

The sound of a gun echoes across the land. An elf screams as her flesh bubbles and her blood boils in the pale glare of a light mage's death ray. The terrible sound of claws and teeth rending flesh can be heard and all that while the metallic smell of blood fills the air. Combat is a perilous and swift affair that will leave you battered and bruised. 

This section provides the rules you need to engage in combat, whether it is a brief skirmish or an extended battle for a settlement. Throughout this section, the rules address you, the player or Game Master. The Game Master controls all the monsters and nonplayer characters involved in combat, and each other player controls themselves, their survivor, as well as any companions they may be assigned. “You” can also mean the character or monster that you control. In the case of conditions or features, it means the creature that "owns" the feature.

Movement and Positioning 

Saturday, January 1, 2022 

6:28 PM 

Movement and Positioning 

In combat, characters and monsters are in constant motion, often using movement and position to gain the upper hand. 

On your turn, you can move a distance up to your speed. You can use as much or as little of your speed as you like on your turn, following the rules here. See the section on Movement in Chapter 7: Basic Survival to see how your speed is determined. 

Your movement can include jumping, climbing, and swimming. These different modes of movement can be combined with walking, or they can constitute your entire move. However you're moving, you deduct the distance of each part of your move from your speed until it is used up or until you are done moving. 

Breaking Up Your Move 

You can break up your movement on your turn, using some of your speed before and after your action. For example, if you have a speed of 10 meters, you can move 5 meters, take your action, and then move 5 meters again. 

Moving Between Attacks 

If you take an action that includes more than one weapon attack, you can break up your movement even further by moving between those attacks. For example, a fighter who can make two attacks with the Extra Attack feature and who has a speed of 10 meters could move 5 meters, make an attack, move 5 meters, and then attack again. 

Using Different Speeds 

If you have more than one speed, such as your walking speed and a flying speed, you can switch back and forth between your speeds during your move. Whenever you switch, subtract the distance you've already moved from the new speed. The result determines how much farther you can move. If the result is 0 or less, you can't use the new speed during the current move. 

For example, if you have a speed of 5 and a flying speed of 15 because you are a harpy, you could fly 2 meters, then walk 3 meters, and then leap into the air to fly another 10 meters. 

Difficult Terrain 

Combat rarely takes place in bare rooms or on featureless plains. A highway slick with oil, briar-choked forests, a snow-covered mountain. All of these can be difficult terrain. 

Every meter of movement in difficult terrain costs an extra meter of speed. This rule is true even if multiple things in a space count as difficult terrain. 

Low furniture, rubble, undergrowth, steep stairs, snow, and shallow bogs are examples of difficult terrain. The space of another creature, whether hostile or not, also counts as difficult terrain. 

Being Prone 

Combatants often find themselves lying on the ground, either because they are knocked down or because they hunker down in cover. In the game, they are prone. You can drop prone as a free action during your turn. Standing up takes more effort; doing so costs an amount of movement equal to half your speed. For example, if your speed is 10 meters, you must spend 5 meters of movement to stand up. You can't stand up if you don't have enough movement left or if your speed is 0. 

To move while prone, you must crawl. Every meter of movement while crawling costs 1 extra meter. Crawling 1 meter in difficult terrain, therefore, costs 3 meters of movement. 

While you are prone, attacks you make have disadvantage, unless the weapon you use is a firearm, a crossbow, or a similar weapon. Ranged attacks against you also have disadvantage due to your low profile, but melee attacks made against you have advantage. 

Moving Around Other Creatures 

You can move through a nonhostile creature's space. You can move through a hostile creature's space only if the creature is at least two sizes larger or smaller than you. Remember that another creature's space is difficult terrain for you. 

Whether a creature is a friend or a foe, you can't willingly end your move in its space.

Combatants often find themselves lying on the ground, either because they are knocked down or because they hunker down in cover. In the game, they are prone. You can drop prone as a free action during your turn. Standing up takes more effort; doing so costs an amount of movement equal to half your speed. You can move through a nonhostile creature's space. You can move through a hostile creature's space only if the creature is at least two sizes larger or smaller than you. Remember that another creature's space is difficult terrain for you. 

Whether a creature is a friend or a foe, you can't willingly end your move in its space. 

If you leave a hostile creature's reach during your move, you provoke an opportunity attack, as explained later in the section. 

Flying Movement 

Flying creatures enjoy many benefits of mobility, but they must also deal with the danger of falling. If a flying creature is knocked prone, has its speed reduced to 0, or is otherwise deprived of the ability to move, the creature falls, unless it can hover. 

  

Creature Size 

Each creature takes up a different amount of space. The Size Categories table shows how much space a creature of a particular size controls in combat. Objects sometimes use the same size categories. The space a creature occupies is measured in squares.  

  

Size Categories 

Size 

	

Space in Meters 

	

Space in Feet 




Tiny 

	

1/4 by 1/4 m. 

	

1.25 by 1.25 ft. 




Small 

	

1/2 by 1/2 m. 

	

2.5 by 2.5 ft. 




Medium 

	

1 by 1 m. 

	

5 by 5 ft. 




Large 

	

2 by 2 m. 

	

10 by 10 ft. 




Huge 

	

5 by 5 m. 

	

15 by 15 ft. 




Gargantuan 

	

10 by 10 m. or larger 

	

30 by 30 ft. or larger 

  

Space 

A creature's space is the area in meters that it effectively controls in combat, not an expression of its physical dimensions. A typical Medium creature isn't 1 meter (5 feet) wide, for example, but it does control a space that wide. If a medium elf stands in a 1-meter (5-feet) wide doorway, other creatures can't get through unless the elf lets them. 

A creature's space also reflects the area it needs to fight effectively. For that reason, there's a limit to the number of creatures that can surround another creature in combat. Assuming medium combatants, eight creatures can fit in a 1-meter (5-foot) radius around another one. 

Because larger creatures take up more space, fewer of them can surround a creature. If four large creatures crowd around a medium or smaller one, there's little room for anyone else. 

  

Squeezing into a Smaller Space 

A creature can squeeze through a space that is large enough for a creature one size smaller than it. Thus, a large creature can squeeze through a passage that's only 1 meter (5 feet) wide. While squeezing through a space, a creature must spend 1 extra meter for every meter it moves there, and it has disadvantage on attack rolls and Dexterity saving throws. Attack rolls against the creature have advantage while it's in the smaller space. 

  

VARIANT: PLAYING ON A GRID 

If you play out a combat using a square grid and miniatures or other tokens, follow these rules. 

Squares. Each square on the grid represents an area of 1 by 1 meter. If you are using feet, each square represents an area of 5 by 5 feet. Some maps may use a different scale. 

Speed. Rather than moving meter by meter, move square by square on the grid.  

Entering a Square. To enter a square, you must have at least 1 square of movement left, even if the square is diagonally adjacent to the square you’re in. 

If a square costs extra movement, as a square of difficult terrain does, you must have enough movement left to pay for entering it. For example, you must have at least 2 squares of movement left to enter a square of difficult terrain. 

Corners. Diagonal movement can’t cross the corner of a wall, large tree, or other terrain feature that fills its space. 

Ranges. To determine the range on a grid between two things—whether creatures or objects—start counting squares from a square adjacent to one of them and stop counting in the space of the other one. Count by the shortest route.

Making an Attack 

Saturday, January 1, 2022 

6:33 PM 

Making an Attack 

Whether you're striking with a melee weapon, firing a firearm at range, or making an attack roll as part of a spell, an attack has a simple structure. 

1. Choose a target. Pick a target within your attack's range: a creature, an object, or a location. 

2. Determine modifiers. The GM determines whether the target has cover and whether you have advantage or disadvantage against the target. In addition, spells, special abilities, and other effects can apply penalties or bonuses to your attack roll. 

3. Resolve the attack. You make the attack roll. On a hit, you roll damage, unless the particular attack has rules that specify otherwise. Some attacks cause special effects in addition to or instead of damage. 

  

Attack Rolls 

When you make an attack, your attack roll determines whether the attack hits or misses. To make an attack roll, roll a d20 and add the appropriate modifiers. If the total of the roll plus modifiers equals or exceeds the target's Armor Class (AC), the attack hits. The AC of a character is determined at character creation, whereas the AC of a monster is in its stat block. 

Modifiers to the Roll 

When a character makes an attack roll, the two most common modifiers to the roll are an ability modifier and the character's proficiency bonus. When a monster makes an attack roll, it uses whatever modifier is provided in its stat block. 

Ability Modifier. The ability modifier used for a melee weapon attack is Strength, and the ability modifier used for a ranged weapon attack is Dexterity. Weapons that have the finesse or thrown property are the exception to this rule. 

Some spells also require an attack roll. The ability modifier used for a spell attack depends on the spellcasting ability of the spellcaster. 

Proficiency Bonus. You add your proficiency bonus to your attack roll when you attack using a weapon with which you have proficiency, as well as when you attack with a spell. 

Rolling 1 or 20 

Sometimes fate blesses or curses a combatant, causing the novice to hit and the veteran to miss. 

If the d20 roll for an attack is a 20, the attack hits regardless of any modifiers or the target's AC. This is called a critical hit, which is explained later in this section. 

If the d20 roll for an attack is a 1, the attack misses regardless of any modifiers or the target's AC. 

Unseen Attackers and Targets 

Combatants often try to escape their foes' notice by hiding, casting the Invisibility spell, or lurking in darkness. 

When you attack a target that you can't see, you have disadvantage on the attack roll. This is true whether you're guessing the target's location or you're targeting a creature you can hear but not see. If the target isn't in the location you targeted, you automatically miss, but the GM typically just says that the attack missed, not whether you guessed the target's location correctly. 

When a creature can't see you, you have advantage on attack rolls against it. If you are hidden, both unseen and unheard, when you make an attack, you give away your location whether the attack hits or misses, even if you are heavily obscured. 

Ranged Attacks 

When you make a ranged attack, you fire a bow or a firearm, hurl a handaxe, or otherwise send projectiles to strike a foe at a distance. Many spells also involve making a ranged attack. 

Range 

You can make ranged attacks only against targets within a specified range. 

If a ranged attack, such as one made with a spell, has a single range, you can't attack a target beyond this range. 

Making an Attack

Making an Attack

3. Resolve the attack. You make the attack roll. On a hit, you roll damage, unless the particular attack has rules that specify otherwise. Some attacks cause special effects in addition to or instead of damage. 

  

Attack Rolls

If the total of the roll plus modifiers equals or exceeds the target's Armor Class (AC), the attack hits. The AC of a character is determined at character creation, whereas the AC of a monster is in its stat block.

Ability Modifier. The ability modifier used for a melee weapon attack is Strength, and the ability modifier used for a ranged weapon attack is Dexterity. Weapons that have the finesse or thrown property are the exception to this rule. 

Some spells also require an attack roll. The ability modifier used for a spell attack depends on the spellcasting ability of the spellcaster. 

Proficiency Bonus. You add your proficiency bonus to your attack roll when you attack using a weapon with which you have proficiency, as well as when you attack with a spell. 

Rolling 1 or 20 

Sometimes fate blesses or curses a combatant, causing the novice to hit and the veteran to miss. 

If the d20 roll for an attack is a 20, the attack hits regardless of any modifiers or the target's AC. This is called a critical hit, which is explained later in this section. 

If the d20 roll for an attack is a 1, the attack misses regardless of any modifiers or the target's AC. 

Unseen Attackers and Targets 

Combatants often try to escape their foes' notice by hiding, casting the Invisibility spell, or lurking in darkness. 

When you attack a target that you can't see, you have disadvantage on the attack roll. This is true whether you're guessing the target's location or you're targeting a creature you can hear but not see. If the target isn't in the location you targeted, you automatically miss, but the GM typically just says that the attack missed, not whether you guessed the target's location correctly. 

When a creature can't see you, you have advantage on attack rolls against it. If you are hidden, both unseen and unheard, when you make an attack, you give away your location whether the attack hits or misses, even if you are heavily obscured. 

Ranged Attacks 

When you make a ranged attack, you fire a bow or a firearm, hurl a handaxe, or otherwise send projectiles to strike a foe at a distance. Many spells also involve making a ranged attack. 

Range 

You can make ranged attacks only against targets within a specified range. 

If a ranged attack, such as one made with a spell, has a single range, you can't attack a target beyond this range. 

Some ranged attacks, such as those made with a bow or a firearm, have two ranges. The smaller number is the normal range, and the larger number is the long range. Your attack roll has disadvantage when your target is beyond normal range, and you can't attack a target beyond the long range. 

Ranged Attacks in Close Combat 

Aiming a ranged attack is more difficult when a foe is next to you. When you make a ranged attack with a weapon, a spell, or some other means, you have disadvantage on the attack roll if you are within 1 meter (5 feet) of a hostile creature that can see you and that isn't incapacitated. 

Melee Attacks 

Used in hand-to-hand combat, a melee attack allows you to attack a foe within your reach. A melee attack typically uses a handheld weapon such as a sword, a warhammer, or an axe. A typical monster makes a melee attack when it strikes with its claws, horns, teeth, tentacles, or other body parts. A few spells also involve making a melee attack. 

Most creatures have a 1-meter (5-foot) reach and can thus attack targets within 1 meter (5 feet) of them when making a melee attack. Certain creatures (typically those larger than Medium) have melee attacks with a greater reach than 1 meter (5 feet), as noted in their descriptions. 

Instead of using a weapon to make a melee weapon attack, you can use an unarmed strike: a punch, kick, head-butt, or similar forceful blow (none of which count as weapons). On a hit, an unarmed strike deals bludgeoning damage equal to 1 + your Strength modifier (minimum 0). You are proficient with your unarmed strikes. 

Attacks of Opportunity 

In a fight, everyone is constantly watching for a chance to strike an enemy who is fleeing or passing by. Such a strike is called an attack of opportunity. 

You can make an attack of opportunity when a hostile creature that you can see moves out of your melee range. To make the opportunity attack, you use your reaction to make one melee attack against the provoking creature. The attack occurs right before the creature leaves your reach. 

You can avoid provoking an opportunity attack by taking the Disengage action. You also don't provoke an opportunity attack when you teleport or when someone or something moves you without using your movement, action, or reaction. For example, you don't provoke an opportunity attack if an explosion hurls you out of a foe's reach or if gravity causes you to fall past an enemy. 

Flanking 

Simultaneous attacks from opposite directions are difficult to defend against. When you and at least one of your allies are adjacent a creature and on opposite sides or corners of that creature’s space, you flank that creature, granting you and all your allies advantage on melee attack rolls against it. If you are a large or larger creature, you qualify for flanking as long as at least one square of your space qualifies for flanking. 

You can only flank a creature that you can see and you cannot flank while incapacitated, such as by the paralyzed condition, or while downed. A creature that is adjacent to one of its allies cannot be flanked from any direction. Vehicles cannot flank or be flanked.  

Two-Weapon Fighting 

When you take the Attack action and attack with a light melee weapon that you're holding in one hand, you can use a bonus action to attack with a different light melee weapon that you're holding in the other hand. You don't add your ability modifier to the damage of the bonus attack, unless that modifier is negative. 

If either weapon has the thrown property, you can throw the weapon, instead of making a melee attack with it. 

Contests in Combat 

Battle often involves pitting your prowess against that of your foe. Such a challenge is represented by a contest. This section includes the most common contests that require an action in combat: grappling and shoving a creature. The GM can use these contests as models for improvising others. 

Grappling 

When you want to grab a creature or wrestle with it, you can use the Attack action to make a special melee attack, a grapple. If you're able to make multiple attacks with the Attack action, this attack replaces one of them. 

Using at least one free hand, you try to seize the target by making a grapple check instead of an attack roll: a Strength (Athletics) check contested by the target's Strength (Athletics) or Dexterity (Acrobatics) check (the target chooses the ability to use). You succeed automatically if the target is incapacitated. If you succeed and the creature is either the same size as you or smaller, you subject the target to the grappled condition.  

Mounting. You may attempt to grapple a creature larger than you. If you succeed, you mount it. Your speed is reduced to 0 and you move with the creature. 

As the grappler, you may end the grapple or dismount a creature whenever you like (no action required). 

Escaping a Grapple. A grappled or mounted creature can use its action to attempt an escape. To do so, it must succeed on a Strength (Athletics) or Dexterity (Acrobatics) check contested by your Strength (Athletics) check. 

Moving a Grappled Creature. When you move, you can drag or carry the grappled creature with you, but your speed is halved, unless the creature is at least one size smaller than you. 

Restraining a Grappled Creature. After subjecting a creature to the grappled condition you may use your action on subsequent turns to restrain the grappled creature until the start of your next turn, or until the grapple ends. 

Shoving a Creature 

Using the Attack action, you can make a special melee attack to shove a creature, either to knock it prone or push it away from you. If you're able to make multiple attacks with the Attack action, this attack replaces one of them. 

The target must be no more than one size larger than you and must be within your reach. Instead of making an attack roll, you make a Strength (Athletics) check contested by the target's Strength (Athletics) or Dexterity (Acrobatics) check (target’s choice). You succeed automatically if the target is incapacitated. If you succeed, you either knock the target prone or push it 1 meter (5 feet) away from you. 

Cover 

Walls, trees, creatures, and other obstacles can provide cover during combat, making a target more difficult to harm. A target can benefit from cover only when an attack or other effect originates on the opposite side of the cover. 

There are three degrees of cover; half, three-quarters and total cover. 

If a target is behind multiple sources of cover, only the most protective degree of cover applies; the degrees aren't added together. For example, if a target is behind a creature that gives half cover and a tree trunk that gives three-quarters cover, the target has three-quarters cover. 

Half Cover 

A target with half cover has a +2 bonus to AC and Dexterity saving throws. A target has half cover if an obstacle blocks at least half of its body. The obstacle might be a low wall, a large piece of furniture, a narrow tree trunk, or a creature, whether that creature is an ally or a foe. 

Three-Quarters Cover 

A target with three-quarters cover has a +5 bonus to AC and Dexterity saving throws. A target has three-quarters cover if about three-quarters of it is covered by an obstacle. The obstacle might be a portcullis, an arrow slit, or a thick tree trunk. 

Total Cover 

A target with total cover can't be targeted directly by an attack or a spell, although some spells can reach such a target by including it in an area of effect. A target has total cover if it is completely concealed by an obstacle, such as a driver sitting in a tank, or a creature behind a wall.
