Monday, September 27, 2021 

4:14 PM 

Damage and Healing 

Combat, injury, and death. Every survivor inevitably encounters all three of these things as they traverse the New World. The claw of an owlbear, a single well-placed bullet, the shock from a Lightning Bolt spell, or the apocalyptic blast from a nuclear weapon or suicide spell. Any of those things may put an end to your journey. 

  

Hit Points (Health) 

Your hit points, also referred to as health, represent a combination of physical and mental durability, the will to live, and the wild magic holding your body together. It is an abstract gauge of how much your body can take before it begins to fail you. Creatures with more hit points are more difficult to kill. They are more powerful, in layman's terms. Those with fewer hit points are more fragile. 

Your current hit points (usually just called HP or health) can be any number between your hit point maximum and  0. This number changes frequently as you take or heal damage. 

Whenever a creature takes damage, that damage is subtracted from its hit points. The loss of hit points has no effect on a creature's capabilities until the creature drops to 0 hit points. Even though you may take a horrible critical hit to the chest that takes you to one hit point, the fact that you have one hit point left means that will, the adrenaline coursing through your veins, wild magic increasing your resilience and luck are still just enough to keep you going. 

As you gain levels, your hit points periodically increase due to a multitude of factors, including training and habit. 

  

Damage Rolls 

Each weapon, spell, and harmful monster ability specifies the damage it deals. You roll the damage die or dice, add any modifiers, and apply the damage to your target. 

With a penalty, it is possible to deal 0 damage, but never negative damage. 

When attacking with a weapon, you often add your ability modifier, the same modifier used for the attack roll, to the damage. Notable exceptions are firearms. A spell tells you which dice to roll for damage and whether to add any modifiers. 

If a spell or other effect deals damage to more than one target at the same time, roll the damage once for all of them. For example, when a mage casts Fireball or a fighter throws a grenade, the damage is rolled once for all creatures caught in the blast. 

Critical Hits 

When you score a critical hit, you roll the attack's damage normally and add the maximum damage the dice could have dealt on top of that. Then you add any relevant modifiers as normal.  

For example, if you score a critical hit with a dagger, roll 1d4 for the damage, add 4 to the result, and then add your relevant ability modifier. If the attack involves other damage dice, such as from the rogue's Sneak Attack feature, you do not maximize and then roll these dice as well, as they are a bonus to the damage of the attack, not a part of it. 

  

Variant: Mild Criticals 

Instead of maximizing the dice, you simply double the number of dice you roll. 

  

Damage Types 

Different attacks, damaging spells, and other harmful effects deal different types of damage. Damage types have only cosmetic effects on their own, but many other mechanics, such as damage resistance, rely on them.  

  

Energy

Combat, injury, and death. Every survivor inevitably encounters all three of these things as they traverse the New World. The claw of an owlbear, a single well-placed bullet, the shock from a Lightning Bolt spell, or the apocalyptic blast from a nuclear weapon or suicide spell. Any of those things may put an end to your journey. 

  

Hit Points (Health) 

Your hit points, also referred to as health, represent a combination of physical and mental durability, the will to live, and the wild magic holding your body together. It is an abstract gauge of how much your body can take before it begins to fail you. Creatures with more hit points are more difficult to kill. They are more powerful, in layman's terms. Those with fewer hit points are more fragile. 

Your current hit points (usually just called HP or health) can be any number between your hit point maximum and  0. This number changes frequently as you take or heal damage. 

Whenever a creature takes damage, that damage is subtracted from its hit points. The loss of hit points has no effect on a creature's capabilities until the creature drops to 0 hit points. Even though you may take a horrible critical hit to the chest that takes you to one hit point, the fact that you have one hit point left means that will, the adrenaline coursing through your veins, wild magic increasing your resilience and luck are still just enough to keep you going. 

As you gain levels, your hit points periodically increase due to a multitude of factors, including training and habit. 

  

Damage Rolls 

Each weapon, spell, and harmful monster ability specifies the damage it deals. You roll the damage die or dice, add any modifiers, and apply the damage to your target. 

With a penalty, it is possible to deal 0 damage, but never negative damage. 

When attacking with a weapon, you often add your ability modifier, the same modifier used for the attack roll, to the damage. Notable exceptions are firearms. A spell tells you which dice to roll for damage and whether to add any modifiers. 

If a spell or other effect deals damage to more than one target at the same time, roll the damage once for all of them. For example, when a mage casts Fireball or a fighter throws a grenade, the damage is rolled once for all creatures caught in the blast. 

Critical Hits 

When you score a critical hit, you roll the attack's damage normally and add the maximum damage the dice could have dealt on top of that. Then you add any relevant modifiers as normal.  

For example, if you score a critical hit with a dagger, roll 1d4 for the damage, add 4 to the result, and then add your relevant ability modifier. If the attack involves other damage dice, such as from the rogue's Sneak Attack feature, you do not maximize and then roll these dice as well, as they are a bonus to the damage of the attack, not a part of it. 

  

Variant: Mild Criticals 

Instead of maximizing the dice, you simply double the number of dice you roll. 

  

Damage Types 

Different attacks, damaging spells, and other harmful effects deal different types of damage. Damage types have only cosmetic effects on their own, but many other mechanics, such as damage resistance, rely on them.  

  

Energy 

These damage types damage a creature by introducing energy into their body. 

Acid 

Acid damage is energy damage caused when the chemical connections holding matter together are broken down. It is usually caused by corrosive or caustic chemicals and magical energy like soulfire. 

A character that takes acid damage has their clothes and skin melted away. A creature that is instantly killed by acid damage falls completely apart, into a skeleton surrounded by a puddle of liquified gore. 

Cold 

Cold damage is energy damage caused by extreme cold. It's usually caused by freezing weather, liquid air, dry ice, or true ice. 

A character that takes cold damage has parts of its body frozen solid. These wounds are painless, but when the dead tissue melts it turns black and blue as it rots. A creature instantly killed by cold damage is frozen solid. 

Fire 

Fire damage is energy damage caused by extreme heat. It is usually inflicted by fire, steam, or plasma (sunfire). 

A character that takes fire damage has their skin blistered and charred black. These wounds are difficult to heal and cause immeasurable pain. A creature that is instantly killed by fire damage is reduced to a charred, unrecognizable corpse. 

Force  

Force damage is energy damage caused by pure kinetic energy being imparted into something. It is usually caused by extreme acceleration, explosions, or force magic. 

A character that takes force damage has their skin turn black and blue from internal injuries. A creature that is instantly killed by force damage is ripped apart. 

Lightning  

Lightning damage is energy damage caused by electrons moving through a medium. It is usually caused by powerful electric currents or intense beams of high energy beta-radiation being fired into a medium and inducing a negative charge while also releasing secondary bremsstrahlung in the form of X-Rays and gamma radiation. 

A character that takes lightning damage receives subtle red, lightning bolt-shaped burns where it was struck. These wounds are usually deep and deal a lot of internal damage that can easily knock someone unconscious. A creature instantly killed by lightning damage has its body blasted apart in a shower of gore as its intestines are vaporized by the attack. 

Necrotic 

Necrotic damage is energy damage caused by cells simply being destroyed inside a living creature's body. It is usually caused by magical effects that draw life force out of a creature. 

A character that takes necrotic damage becomes pale, their features sink and sickly green and blue spots appear on their body. A creature instantly killed by necrotic damage shrivels into a gaunt figure. 

Poison 

Poison damage is damage caused by harmful substances that damage by interfering with vital bodily functions.  

What effect this takes depends on the poison and is detailed in the poison's description (In case of a creature, in their behavior section). 

Psychic 

Psychic damage is damage caused by disturbing the connection between a soul and its body. It is usually caused by divine magic or extreme psychological distress.  

A character that takes psychic damage bleeds from their eyes and nose, twitches, and vomits. 

Radiant 

Radiant damage is energy damage caused by intense radiation. It is usually dealt by bursts of UV or gamma radiation or other extremely intense light. 

A character that takes radiant damage has their skin burned and blistered, or holes blown into their body by intense beams. A creature instantly killed by radiant damage is reduced to a blistered corpse that rapidly turns blue and black. 

Thunder  

Thunder damage is energy damage caused by sonic waves that vibrate the body in harmful ways. It is usually caused by sonic attacks.  

A character that takes thunder damage has their skin turn black and blue from internal injuries and bleeds from their ears and nose. A creature that is instantly killed by thunder damage ist torn apart. 

  

Physical 

These types damage a creature through physical interaction. 

Bludgeoning 

Bludgeoning damage is physical damage that is inflicted when a blunt object is slammed into something else. It is usually caused by hammers, clubs, unarmed attacks, or by falling off a cliff.  

A character that takes bludgeoning damage will have broken bones and bruises that only bleed lightly if at all but hurt a lot. If a creature is instantly killed by bludgeoning damage it is smashed to a pulp, turning it into a mass of gore and broken bones. 

Piercing 

Piercing damage is physical damage that is inflicted when a sharp/small object penetrates something else. This type of damage is usually inflicted by stabbing weapons or firearms. 

A character that takes piercing damage will bleed lightly from small holes in their body. If a creature is instantly killed by piercing damage their head is completely pierced and ripped apart by the attack. 

Slashing 

Slashing damage is physical damage that is inflicted when a sharp object slices through something to create a narrow cut. This type of damage is usually inflicted by bladed weapons. 

A character that receives slashing damage will bleed profusely from cuts and lacerations all over their body. If a creature is instantly killed by slashing damage, they are cut in half. 

  

Armor Piercing Damage 

A damage type can have the Armor Piercing sub-type. Armor Piercing attacks ignore the bonus AC granted to a target by its armor and can damage some enemies that are immune to normal attacks, like tanks. For example, the target of an armor-piercing attack would have a Base AC of 10 but the plate armor it is wearing increases its AC to 18. The plus 8 bonus is ignored if the attack has the armor-piercing property. If the armor the creature is wearing doesn't allow it to add its Dexterity modifier to its AC, the attack ignores it as well. 

  

Damage Resistance, Immunity and Vulnerability 

Some creatures and objects are exceedingly difficult, impossible, or unusually easy to hurt with certain damage types. It is usually one of the damage types described above that a creature is resistant, immune, or vulnerable to, but it may also be any of those things against a certain source of damage. For example, a tank (vehicle) is immune to damage that isn't from an armor-piercing attack. 

If a creature or an object has resistance to a kind of damage, damage of that kind is halved against it.  

If a creature or an object has vulnerability to a kind of damage, damage of that kind is doubled against it.  

If a creature is immune to a kind of damage, any damage of that kind is multiplied by zero. 

Resistance and then vulnerability are applied after all other modifiers to damage. For example, a creature has resistance to piercing damage and is hit by an attack that deals 15 piercing damage. The creature is also within a magical aura that reduces all damage by 5. The 15 damage is first reduced by 5 and then halved, so the creature has its current hit points reduced by 5. 

Multiple instances of resistance or vulnerability that affect the same instance of damage do not stack. A creature that is immune to a certain damage type and becomes vulnerable to it takes no damage, unless the effect that makes it vulnerable explicitly bypasses or removes its immunity. 

  

Healing 

Unless it results in death, damage isn't permanent and even a recent death is reversible through powerful magic. Rest can restore a creature's hit points, and magical methods such as a healing spell or a healing potion can remove damage almost instantly. However, most effects rely on your own body's ability to repair itself and there is a limit to how much material your body has available for such repairs. This material is the same thing that keeps your body running and active. As you start to run low on these organic compounds, your bodily functions are inhibited, something you experience as exhaustion. 

This 'material' is represented by your hit dice. Almost all abilities that provide healing, and some others, require you to spend hit-dice to receive their benefits. 

When you spend all your available hit dice, you gain a level of exhaustion (see the condition in Appendix A) and the number of hit dice you have is reset to your maximum, allowing you to continue spending them. 

When a creature receives healing of any kind, hit points regained are added to its current hit points. A creature's hit points can't exceed its hit point maximum, so any hit points healed in excess of this number are lost. 

A creature that has died can't regain hit points until magic such as the Revivify spell has restored it to life. While it is dead, a creature's corpse is considered an object. 

  

Dropping to 0 Hit Points 

When you drop to 0 hit points, you either die instantly or get downed, as explained in the following sections. 

Instant Death 

Massive damage can kill you instantly. When damage reduces you to 0 hit points and there is damage remaining, you die if the remaining damage equals or exceeds your hit point maximum. 

For example, Larissa, a prophet with a maximum of 12 hit points currently has 6 hit points. If she takes 18 damage from an attack, she is reduced to 0 hit points, but 12 damage remains. Because the remaining damage equals her hit point maximum, Larissa dies instantly. 

Down But Not Out 

If damage reduces you to 0 hit points and fails to kill you instantly, you gain a level of exhaustion, a lingering injury, and are downed.   

When downed, you fall prone, are incapacitated, and can't get up until you are no longer downed. You remain downed until you either regain hit points, or die. 

While downed you can no longer take normal actions. Instead, you can dedicate your entire turn to doing one of the following things: 

Move up to your speed 

Take the hide action. 

Give yourself advantage on your next death saving throw. 

Drink a potion.  

Pretend to be dead by making a Charisma (Deception) check against the passive Perception of those you are trying to fool. 

You can take no other actions, bonus actions, or reactions unless the action's description specifically states that it can be used while downed, in which case performing it always requires your entire turn. After spending your turn to perform one of the aforementioned, or any other special action, your turn automatically ends, even if you managed to end the downed condition. 

Lingering Injuries 

When an attack reduces you to 0 hit points, it means that the damage has overwhelmed you and caused some sort of trauma that your body can no longer just shrug off. Even if you recover from it, it will likely leave you with more than a minor scar. This damage is represented by a lingering injury and can't be healed through normal means. The damage is rooted so deep that your body's natural ability to regenerate is insufficient to recover from it. Removing it either requires powerful magic like the Regenerate ability of Channel Life II, a modern medical professional, or both. 

A lingering injury can be anything from a broken nose that needs to be reset, to a debilitating brain injury and is determined by your GM, either randomly, or from the circumstances surrounding your injury. The kind of injury you receive depends on the damage type of the attack that reduced you to 0 hit points. Note any lingering injuries you are suffering from on your character sheet. 

Death Saving Throws 

Whenever you end your turn with 0 hit points, you must make a special saving throw, called a death saving throw, to determine whether you creep closer to death or hang onto life. Unlike other saving throws, this one isn't tied to any ability score. You are in the hands of fate now, aided only by spells and features that improve your chances of succeeding on a saving throw. 

Roll a d20. If the roll is 10 or higher, you succeed. Otherwise, you fail. On your third success, you become stable (see below).  

Failing a death save while downed means you fall unconscious until you are no longer downed. On your third failure, you die. The successes and failures don't need to be consecutive; keep track of both until you collect three of a kind. The number of both is reset to zero when you regain any hit points or become stable. 

Downed by a critical hit. If damage from a critical hit downs you, you automatically fail a death saving throw as part of being downed. This also means you fall unconscious immediately after being reduced to 0 hit points and won't be able to act. 

Rolling 1 or 20. When you make a death saving throw and roll a 1 on the d20, it counts as two failures. If you roll a 20 on the d20, you regain 1 hit point. 

Damage at 0 Hit Points. If you take any damage while you have 0 hit points, you suffer a death saving throw failure. If the damage is from a critical hit, you suffer two failures instead. If the damage equals or exceeds your hit point maximum, you die. 

Stabilizing a Creature 

The best way to save a creature with 0 hit points is to heal it. If healing is unavailable, the creature can at least be stabilized so that it isn't killed by a failed death saving throw. 

You can use your action to administer first aid to an unconscious creature and attempt to stabilize it, which requires a successful DC 10 Wisdom (Medicine) check. 

A stable creature doesn't make death saving throws, even though it has 0 hit points, but it does remain downed. The creature stops being stable, and must start making death saving throws again, if it takes any damage.  

Monsters and Death 

By default NPCs are governed by the same rules. For simplicities sake, the GM may make a single death saving throw immediately when the creature is downed. On a success the creature becomes stable, on a failure it dies instantly. 

  

Temporary Hit Points 

Some spells and special abilities confer temporary hit points to a creature. Temporary hit points aren't actual hit points; they are a buffer against damage, a pool of hit points that protects you from injury. 

When you have temporary hit points and take damage, the temporary hit points are lost first, and any leftover damage carries over to your normal hit points. For example, if you have 5 temporary hit points and take 7 damage, you lose the temporary hit points and then take 2 damage. 

Because temporary hit points are separate from your actual hit points, they can exceed your hit point maximum.  

Healing can't restore temporary hit points, and they can't be added together. If you have temporary hit points and receive more of them, you decide whether to keep the ones you have or to gain the new ones. For example, if a spell grants you 12 temporary hit points when you already have 10, you can have 12 or 10, not 22. 

If you have 0 hit points, receiving temporary hit points doesn't restore you to consciousness or stabilize you. They can still absorb damage directed at you while you're in that state, but only true healing can save you.  

Unless a feature that grants you temporary hit points has a duration, they last until they're depleted or you finish a long rest. 

 

 

 

 

 

 

 

Archive 

Healing Example: 

For example Tim is an elven level 5 Life Mage. He has 1d8 and 5d6 hit die (1d8 from his race and 5d6 from his class). A sniper has shot him in the chest, taking him from full 25 health down to 3. Tim uses the spell Healing to instantly spend 4 hit-dice for a burst of regeneration. He recovers 1d8 and 3d6 for a total of 13 health, putting him back up to 16. On his next round he casts to spell again, but he only has a single 1d6 hit-die left. He heals for 3 health, before gaining a level of exhaustion and then spending another 2 hit dice to get himself back to full health.

Mounted Combat 

Saturday, January 1, 2022 

8:16 PM 

Mounted Combat 

A soldier riding circles around his foes, firing from atop their trusty steed. A mage peppering the enemy position with lightning bolts from the safety of his flying gripper mount. An imp riding atop the shoulders of his ally. All enjoy the benefits of speed and mobility only a mount can provide. 

A willing creature that is at least one size larger than you and that has an appropriate anatomy can serve as a mount, using the following rules. 

Mounting and Dismounting 

Mounting a creature requires you to be within 1 meter (5 feet) of the creature you wish to mount. You then use either your action or half your movement to mount the creature. For example, if your speed is 10 meters, you must either use your action or spend 5 meters of movement to mount a horse. You cannot mount a creature if you are incapacitated, or restrained. 

Dismounting requires no action and may be done at any time. However, since your mount replaces your speed while you are mounted, if you moved using your mount this turn, you will not be able to move after dismounting (unless you take the Dash action).  

If an effect moves your mount against its will while you’re on it, you must succeed on a DC 10 Dexterity saving throw or fall off the mount, landing prone in a space within 1 meter (5 feet) of it. If you’re knocked prone while mounted, you must make the same saving throw. If your speed is reduced to 0 while mounted, you are also dismounted and fall prone. 

Controlling a Mount 

While you’re mounted, you have two options. You can either control the mount or allow it to act independently. Intelligent creatures, such as dragons or your allies, act independently. If the creature you are riding is an animal companion, it will obey your commands, but can still take actions as if it were an independent creature. 

You can control a mount only if it has been trained to accept a rider. Domesticated horses, donkeys, and any pet you have will accept you as a rider. While mounted on it, your speed is replaced with the mount's speed and the mount can take no actions of its own. It takes its turn with you and does as you direct it. 

An independent mount retains its place in the initiative order. Bearing a rider puts no restrictions on the actions the mount can take, and it moves and acts as it wishes (though in case of a pet, it will still act with the character it is assigned to). It might flee from combat, rush to attack and devour a badly injured foe, or otherwise act against your wishes. 

In either case, if the mount provokes an opportunity attack while you’re on it, the attacker can target you or the mount.

Chapter 10: The Rules of Spellcasting 

Tuesday, March 2, 2021 

8:50 PM 

Chapter 10: Spellcasting 

Our world is now suffused with magic. It has seeped into the very fabric of the land, the air, and all living things. Each character class has a unique approach to learning and preparing their spells, while monsters have their own unique ways of harnessing their powers. Regardless of where the spell comes from, its effects and limitations are dictated by the rules in this chapter. 

 

 

 

Upcasting a spell makes it more difficult to cast and the number of times you may upcast a spell equals your Proficiency Bonus. This means that starting at 1st level you may upcast your spells twice. Starting at 5th level you may upcast your spells  

When building a spellcaster, the most important choice to make is what lore you have access to as that both determines your abilities and your most important ability scores. See the Lores of Magic able below for a brief description of the available lores, their playstyles and the ability scores that govern their use. Below is a brief summary of the rules on spellcasting.

Monday, July 18, 2022 

2:58 PM 

What Is A Spell? 

A spell is a magical effect, manifested by drawing and then releasing arcane energy using a very specific, limited technique. In its simplest form, magic is nothing more than a soul converting vitality into energy of some kind. When you cast a spell, you reach into yourself and pull energy from your body and convert it into an arcane effect. Once you are done drawing and converting the needed vitality, the spell cast is complete and you can now hold the energy or unleash it at your leisure.  

In theory, any spellcaster can manifest this energy any way he wants and draw as much as his body can manage at once. However, summoning a magical effect is an incredibly complicated process that requires precise martial and mental techniques that need to be executed flawlessly to eliminate the risk of the spell fizzling, misfiring, or just consuming more energy than it should. Combat or similarly stressful situations are a terrible place to take your time and focus, which is why people who expect to be forced to use magic under such conditions train specific, optimized techniques that create a certain effect with the least amount of vitality possible. These techniques are known as spells. Spellcasters train these spells until the techniques involved become ingrained knowledge. 

Mastering a spell in a way that lets you cast it at a moment's notice takes months or even years, but if your patron was a spellcaster, you have a shortcut. Your patron's ingrained knowledge is at your disposal, as long as you have some way to unlock it. Ingrained knowledge like that needed to walk is unlocked when you take your first steps in your new body, but the ability to cast spells takes a little bit more. As you gain experience, you unlock more and more knowledge until you reach the level of ability your patron had when their knowledge was copied to you. 

  

  

Spell Levels 

Spells are usually categorized into five categories of complexity. Novice, Apprentice, Adept, Expert, and Master-level spells. Instead of saying the full name, the categories are also often described as levels, with Novice level spells being 1st level spells and Master 5th level spells. Every spell falls into one of these categories. A spell's level is an indicator of how difficult the techniques it requires are to perform. When a mage gains the ability to learn spells of a higher level, it means that they have learned a new technique that allows them to channel energy into a new form of their element. This new ability is represented by the Channel spells. Spells of a specific level then use this new form or way of channeling that energy to create specific effects. For example, when a fire mage gains the ability to learn apprentice-level spells, it means that they are now experienced enough to create stable sunfire, instead of just normal heat. This new capability is represented by the Channel Fire II spell. On its own, this ability allows them to create tiny specks of sunfire, but other apprentice level spells then build on that. For example, heat is channeled into the form of sunfire, stabilized, and can then be unleashed as the Fireball spell.

Monday, July 18, 2022 

2:58 PM 

Casting Spells 

When a character casts any spell, the same basic rules are followed, regardless of the character’s class or the spell’s effects. While maneuvers are not spells, the rules that apply to spells apply to maneuvers as well unless stated otherwise. 

Each spell’s description begins with a block of information, including the spell’s name, level, lore, casting time, range, and duration. This summary is followed by a prose description of how the spell is physically cast and its (visual) effects. Finally, the mechanics of the spell are defined. 

  

Somatics 

Spells require at least one free hand and the ability to move more or less freely to cast properly, as the martial movements described in a spell are needed to free the flow of energy in your body. Casting a spell without a free hand (either because you are holding something or the limb was amputated), or while you are restrained doubles that spell's vitality cost. 

  

Casting Time 

Most Spells require a single Action to cast, but some spells require a bonus Action, a reaction, or much more time to cast. 

  

Bonus Action 

A spell cast with a bonus action is especially swift. You may use a bonus action on your turn to cast the spell. If you've already used a your bonus action, you must use your action to cast the spell instead. 

  

Reactions 

Some spells can be cast as reactions. These spells take a fraction of a second to complete and are cast in response to an event of your choice. Most spells that may be cast as a reaction are particularly, or only, effective when cast as a reaction to an event specified in the spell's description. 

  

Longer Casting Times 

Certain Spells require more time to cast: minutes or even hours. When you cast a spell with a casting time longer than a single action or reaction, you must spend your action each turn casting the spell, and you must maintain your concentration while you do so (see “Concentration” below). If your concentration is broken, the spell fails and any vitality committed to it is lost. If you want to try casting the spell again, you must start over. 

  

Vitality 

Vitality is your physical energy. The same energy you use to move, think and live. When you cast a spell, this physical energy is drained out of your muscles and organs and converted into energy. As your vitality supply gets lower, you feel more and more tired. Theoretically, using up all your vitality would kill you but your body has a failsafe to guard against such an outcome. Your vitality score represents the amount your body can spare, not how much it has available. When this pool is exhausted and your current vitality becomes 0, your body pulls the plug and you fall unconscious. You remain unconscious for the amount of time it takes your species to take a night's rest, or until you regain vitality. When you fall unconscious, you gain 1 level of exhaustion. 

Vitality Cost. The vitality a spell requires is detailed in the spell description. When you begin casting a spell, reduce your current vitality by the amount specified in the spell. Some spells have a per turn cost. In this case, you expend the listed amount when you first cast the spell. To maintain the spell you must expend the specified amount at the start of the time period described in the spell's description. You may end such a spell at any time, no action required. 

 

Monday, July 18, 2022 

2:58 PM 

Casting Spells 

When a character casts any spell, the same basic rules are followed, regardless of the character’s class or the spell’s effects. While maneuvers are not spells, the rules that apply to spells apply to maneuvers as well unless stated otherwise. 

Each spell’s description begins with a block of information, including the spell’s name, level, lore, casting time, range, and duration. This summary is followed by a prose description of how the spell is physically cast and its (visual) effects. Finally, the mechanics of the spell are defined. 

  

Somatics 

Spells require at least one free hand and the ability to move more or less freely to cast properly, as the martial movements described in a spell are needed to free the flow of energy in your body. Casting a spell without a free hand (either because you are holding something or the limb was amputated), or while you are restrained doubles that spell's vitality cost. 

  

Casting Time 

Most Spells require a single Action to cast, but some spells require a bonus Action, a reaction, or much more time to cast. 

  

Bonus Action 

A spell cast with a bonus action is especially swift. You may use a bonus action on your turn to cast the spell. If you've already used a your bonus action, you must use your action to cast the spell instead. 

  

Reactions 

Some spells can be cast as reactions. These spells take a fraction of a second to complete and are cast in response to an event of your choice. Most spells that may be cast as a reaction are particularly, or only, effective when cast as a reaction to an event specified in the spell's description. 

  

Longer Casting Times 

Certain Spells require more time to cast: minutes or even hours. When you cast a spell with a casting time longer than a single action or reaction, you must spend your action each turn casting the spell, and you must maintain your concentration while you do so (see “Concentration” below). If your concentration is broken, the spell fails and any vitality committed to it is lost. If you want to try casting the spell again, you must start over. 

  

Vitality 

Vitality is your physical energy. The same energy you use to move, think and live. When you cast a spell, this physical energy is drained out of your muscles and organs and converted into energy. As your vitality supply gets lower, you feel more and more tired. Theoretically, using up all your vitality would kill you but your body has a failsafe to guard against such an outcome. Your vitality score represents the amount your body can spare, not how much it has available. When this pool is exhausted and your current vitality becomes 0, your body pulls the plug and you fall unconscious. You remain unconscious for the amount of time it takes your species to take a night's rest, or until you regain vitality. When you fall unconscious, you gain 1 level of exhaustion. 

Vitality Cost. The vitality a spell requires is detailed in the spell description. When you begin casting a spell, reduce your current vitality by the amount specified in the spell. Some spells have a per turn cost. In this case, you expend the listed amount when you first cast the spell. To maintain the spell you must expend the specified amount at the start of the time period described in the spell's description. You may end such a spell at any time, no action required. 

Negligible Cost. When a spell has a negligible vitality cost, it means that the degree to which this spell tires you out is negligible in the heat of combat. However, it does not mean using it does not tire you at all. Using such a spell is comparable to a brisk jog. You can cast it continuously for hours, but not while resting or all day without suffering exhaustion. 

   

Upcasting 

The spells casters learn today are techniques that masters have developed and refined as time passed. A spell is the most efficient and stable way to summon a certain effect. However, spellcasters are not limited to casting efficiently or safely and may decide that they require more firepower. When they do, they may expend additional vitality to upcast their spell. However, handling greater energy flows requires skill and is a gamble. 

Spells that can be upcast have a section marked Upcast in their description. It details how much additional vitality must be expended to gain a perceivable increase in effectiveness and what effect spending this additional vitality has. To upcast a spell, you first pay the base vitality cost, then the first upcast cost. You then make an Upcast Check. Roll a d20 and add your character level + proficiency bonus + spellcasting ability modifier to the result. 

The DC for this check is 10 for the first upcast level, and increases by 5 for each additional upcast of the same spell. 

On a successful check, you may either choose to cast the spell, or upcast it further by paying the upcast cost again and then making another upcast check. On a failed check, the spell fizzles. A fizzled spell has no effect, the vitality paid is lost and the action used to cast the spell is wasted. On a natural 1, the spell misfires. In addition to the spell failing to cast, you take damage equal to the vitality expended. The damage type depends on the lore of the spell, see the Misfire Damage Type table. The only exception is divine magic, which deals no damage to the caster on a misfire. 

 

Lore 

	

Damage Type 




Lore of Force 

	

Force 




Lore of Light 

	

Radiant 




Lore of Frost 

	

Cold 




Lore of Lightning 

	

Lightning 




Lore of Fire 

	

Fire 




Lore of Life 

	

Acid 




Divine 

	

None 

  

Downcasting 

Casters can always choose to make their spells weaker. They may decrease the number of damage dice they roll or make the effect of their spell smaller. However, doing so only has a negligible effect on the amount of vitality that must be expended to summon the spell in the first place. 

  

Final Push 

Your vitality cannot be reduced below 0. If casting a spell, initiating a maneuver, or using another ability that requires vitality would reduce you to or below 0 vitality, you are instead reduced to 0 vitality, the spell or ability fizzles and you fall unconscious. Instead of having the spell fail, you may attempt to go beyond your limits with a final push. You make a Constitution saving throw with a DC equal to the amount casting that spell or using that ability would bring you into the negative. On a failed save, you fall unconscious and the spell fails. On a success, the spell succeeds and is cast as normal. You then reduce your current hit points by an amount equal to the vitality you were unable to expend and gain 1 level of exhaustion. You then fall unconscious. 

 

Note: The Void 

Those from the Other World say that magic originates from something called the Void. It is also the place the gods reside in, the place from which the universe spawned, the place the fog originates from, and the thing that connects everything. So what is the Void? The phrase is used to describe the unexplained. It is not a place or even a physical phenomenon, but a placeholder. There are many things that simply cannot be explained. The sages, philosophers, and scientists of the Other World accepted the fact that some things are beyond their comprehension. They figured that some things simply come from nothing and the translation for the word that describes this nothing is: The Void. 

Even though master mages know exactly how magic works, they do not know why it works. Since the origin of magic is unknown, it comes from the Void. To provide a modern example, we know exactly how gravity works. We can calculate the exact gravitational pull an object generates and predict how gravity will affect it. However, we do not know why amounts of mass attract each other. Thus gravity would come from the Void. 

  

Range 

The target of a spell must be within the spell’s range. For a spell like Whisper of Pain, the target is a creature. For a spell like Fireball, the target is the point in space where the spell’s projectile detonates. 

Most Spells have ranges expressed in meters. If you are using feet, multiply the provided number by 3 and round down to the nearest 5 to determine the spell’s range in feet. Some spells can target only a creature (including you) that you touch. Other spells, such as the Shield spell, affect only you. These spells have a range of self. 

Spells that create cones or lines of effects that originate from you also have a range of self, indicating that the origin point of the spell’s effect must be you (see “Areas of Effect”). In these cases, the word self is followed by the type and size of the area of effect in brackets. 

Once a spell is cast, its effects aren’t limited by its range, unless the spell’s description says otherwise. 

  

Duration 

A spell’s duration is the length of time the spell persists. A duration can be expressed in rounds, minutes, hours, or even years. Some spells specify that their effects last until the spells are dispelled. If a spell effect is permanent, there is no way to end, undue, or dispel its effects. 

Instantaneous 

Many Spells are instantaneous. The spell harms, heals, creates, or alters a creature or an object in a way that can’t be dispelled because its magic exists only for an instant. 

Concentration 

Some Spells require you to maintain concentration in order to keep their magic active. If you lose concentration, such a spell ends. 

If a spell must be maintained with concentration, that fact appears in its duration entry, and the spell specifies how long you can concentrate on it. You can end concentration at any time (no action required). 

Normal activity, such as moving, casting other spells, and attacking, doesn’t interfere with concentration. The following factors can break concentration: 

Casting another spell that requires concentration. You lose Concentration on a spell if you cast another spell that requires Concentration. You can’t concentrate on two Spells at once. 

Taking Damage. Whenever you take damage while you are concentrating on a spell, you must make a Constitution saving throw to maintain your concentration. The DC equals 10. If you take damage from multiple sources, such as an arrow and a dragon’s breath, you make a separate saving throw for each source of damage. 

Being incapacitated, downed, or killed. You lose concentration on a spell if you are incapacitated, downed, or if you die. Some spells specify that you are incapacitated while concentrating on them. These spells only end if you would be incapacitated by an effect other than the spell you are concentrating on. 

The GM might also decide that certain environmental phenomena, such as the floor falling away under you in a collapsing building, require you to succeed on a DC 10 Constitution saving throw to maintain Concentration on a spell. 

  

Targets 

A typical spell requires you to pick one or more targets to be affected by it. A spell’s description tells you whether the spell targets Creatures, Objects, or a point of origin for an area of effect (described below). 

Silent Spells 

Most spells make a lot of noise when cast, or have flashy effects that reveal the caster's presence to anyone nearby. A spell that is silent makes no noise when cast and so does not reveal the caster's location to nearby creatures, but if a creature can see the caster it is still obvious that they are casting a spell. 

Subtle Spells 

Most spells have obvious visual cues that make it obvious the caster is preparing to cast a spell or has just done so. Spells that are subtle have no or very subtle effects, making it difficult to detect that they are being cast. A creature that is specifically paying attention to the caster must succeed a Wisdom (Perception) check against their spell save DC to detect that they are preparing to cast a spell. The focus needed for this is usually not possible in active combat and subtle spells cannot be countered using reactions.  

Unless it is also silent, casting a subtle spell still reveals the caster when the spell is released. 

A Clear Path to the Target 

To target something, you must have a clear path to it, so it can’t be behind total cover. 

If you place an area of effect at a point that you can’t see and an obstruction, such as a wall, is between you and that point, the point of origin comes into being on the near side of that obstruction. 

Targeting Yourself 

If a spell targets a creature of your choice, you can choose yourself, unless the creature must be hostile or specifically a creature other than you.  

Areas of Effect 

Spells such as Burn and Freeze cover an area, allowing them to affect multiple creatures at once. Unless an area of effect spell has a range of self, indicating you as the source of the effect, if you are in the area of effect of a spell you cast, you affect yourself unless the spell allows you to choose your targets or specifies otherwise. 

A spell’s description specifies its area of effect, which typically has one of five different shapes: cone, cube, cylinder, line, or sphere. Every area of effect has a point of origin, the place upon which you center the area of effect. The rules for each shape specify how you position its point of origin. Typically, a point of origin is a point in space, but some spells have an area whose origin is a creature or an object. 

A spell’s effect expands in straight lines from the point of origin. If no unblocked straight line extends from the point of origin to a location within the area of effect, that location isn’t affected. To block one of these imaginary lines, an obstruction must provide total cover. 

Cone 

A cone extends in a direction you choose from its point of origin. A cone’s width at a given point along its length is equal to that point’s distance from the point of Origin. A cone’s area of effect specifies its maximum length. A cone’s point of Origin is not included in the cone’s area of effect unless you wish it to be. 

Cube 

You select a cube’s point of origin, which lies anywhere on a face of the cubic effect. The cube’s size is expressed as the length of each side. A cube’s point of origin is not included in the cube’s area of effect unless you wish it to be. 

Cylinder 

A cylinder’s point of origin is the center of a circle of a particular radius, as given in the spell description. The circle must either be on the ground or at the height of the spell effect. The energy in a cylinder expands in straight lines from the point of origin to the perimeter of The Circle, forming the base of the cylinder. The spell’s effect then shoots up from the base or down from the top, to a distance equal to the height of the Cylinder. 

A cylinder’s point of origin is included in the cylinder’s area of effect. 

Line 

A line extends from its point of origin in a straight path up to its length and covers an area defined by its width. 

A line’s point of Origin is not included in the line’s area of effect, unless you wish it to be. 

Sphere 

You select a sphere’s point of origin, and the sphere extends outward from that point. The sphere’s size is expressed as a radius in meters that extends from the point. A sphere’s point of origin is included in the sphere’s area of effect. 

  

Flavor Text 

A spell's flavor text describes the movements your character makes when casting the spell and the visual effects of the spell. It also often provides a visual description of the spell's optimal effects. 

Flavor text is meant to help players (and GMs) describe the effects of their spells. It has no direct mechanical meaning but may be used to interpret the mechanical description of the spell. 

  

Saving Throws 

Many Spells specify that a target can make a saving throw to avoid some or all of a spell’s effects. The spell specifies the ability that the target uses for the save and what happens on a success or failure. 

The DC to resist one of your spells equals 8 + your Spellcasting Ability Modifier + your Proficiency Bonus + any Special Modifiers. Your spellcasting ability modifier is usually determined by the type of magic you are using. Every type of magic describes how the saving throws of its spells are determined. 

  

Attack Rolls 

Some spells require the caster to make an attack roll to determine whether the spell effect hits the intended target. Your attack bonus with a Spell Attack equals your Spellcasting Ability Modifier + your Proficiency Bonus. 

Most spells that require attack rolls involve ranged attacks. Remember that you have disadvantage on a ranged attack roll if you are within 1 meter (5 feet) of a hostile creature that can see you and that isn’t incapacitated. 

  

Combining Magical Effects 

The effects of different spells add together while the durations of those spells overlap. The effects of the same spell cast multiple times don’t combine. Instead, the most potent effect, such as the highest bonus, from those castings applies while their durations overlap. 

 

The Schools of Magic 

There are several different ways how magical energy can be manifested, all based around radically different approaches to the arcane. There is divine magic, elemental magic, divided into six lores (force, light, frost, lightning, fire, and life), wild magic, and witchcraft, split into potion formulas and enchantments. Finally, there are maneuvers. Maneuvers are not magic, but follow similar rules with their use and are described here for simplicities sake. 

Every school of magic has special rules unique to its spells. At the beginning of a schools subchapter you will find a section detailing these special rules. Remember, specific beats general, meaning that a school's rules contradict the previously established rules, they have priority. 

 

The Five Spell Levels 

There are five distinct stages at which a mage's abilities develop. These stages are called novice, apprentice, adept, expert, and master. As a novice, a spellcaster has just learned to summon energy, and specialized spells of that skill level focus on directing the summoned, unstable energy in useful ways. Once a mage gains some experience, they learn to focus and stabilize their element, usually into a new form, making them an apprentice. Next, they will work on increasing the amount of power they can safely draw and stabilize until they become adept. Expert spells require that the spellcaster can summon large amounts of focused energy very quickly and finally, a master must be able to project their elemental energy and forcefully convert physical matter into it. This will give them access to devastating suicide-spells and other powerful abilities. 

 

 

 

 

 

Archive 

Upcasting 

The spells casters learn today are techniques that masters have developed and refined as time passed. A spell is the most efficient way to summon a certain effect. However, spellcasters are not limited to casting efficiently and may decide that they require more firepower. When they do, they may expend additional vitality to upcast their spell, however handling greater energy flows requires skill. 

Spells that can be upcast have a section called Upcast in their description. It details how much additional vitality must be expended to gain a perceivable increase in effectiveness and what effect spending this additional vitality has. 

The number of times you may upcast a spell equals your Proficiency Bonus. 

 

Spellcasting in Heavy Armor 

Magic causes the wielder's body to generate massive amounts of excess heat, meaning that thick clothing that covers the entire body and cannot be easily taken off can quickly cause the caster's body to overheat. 

While you are wearing heavy armor, double the vitality cost of your spells (if another feature would double the cost of a spell or maneuver, this penalty triples it instead).

Chapter 10: The Rules of Spellcasting 

Tuesday, March 2, 2021 

8:50 PM 

Chapter 10: Spellcasting 

Our world is now suffused with magic. It has seeped into the very fabric of the land, the air, and all living things. Each character class has a unique approach to learning and preparing their spells, while monsters have their own unique ways of harnessing their powers. Regardless of where the spell comes from, its effects and limitations are dictated by the rules in this chapter. 

 

 

 

Upcasting a spell makes it more difficult to cast and the number of times you may upcast a spell equals your Proficiency Bonus. This means that starting at 1st level you may upcast your spells twice. Starting at 5th level you may upcast your spells  

When building a spellcaster, the most important choice to make is what lore you have access to as that both determines your abilities and your most important ability scores. See the Lores of Magic able below for a brief description of the available lores, their playstyles and the ability scores that govern their use. Below is a brief summary of the rules on spellcasting.

Chapter 11: Arias (27) 

Tuesday, May 3, 2022 

5:59 PM 

Chapter 11: Arias 

Arias are a form of magic that uses the harmonics and frequencies of music to amplify and draw out the energy that flows through the surrounding area. This unique approach to magic allows arias to affect multiple creatures at once, making them an ideal choice when fighting with many allies or against many foes. More importantly, since you usually need to awaken the magic within your intended targets for the aria to take effect, you may choose which creatures to affect and which to exclude from your arias. 

The full potential of an aria can only be realized with sufficient time and focus, which can be a challenging task in the midst of battle. 

  

  

Aria magic relies on your Charisma score 

•The Difficulty Class of saving throws against aria spells you cast is determined as follows: 8 + your Proficiency Bonus + your Charisma modifier.  

•If an aria requires an attack roll, then the modifier for that roll equals: your Proficiency Bonus + Your Charisma modifier.  

  

Arias are spells. Beginning an aria is usually an action, but may also only require a bonus action or a reaction. The time required is listed as the aria's casting time. Regardless of the aria's casting time, when you use it, you begin casting its 1st verse. Refer to the spell's description to see the effects of this verse. All arias increase in power the longer they are maintained. Maintaining an aria requires concentration. You are revealed (see the condition) while concentrating on an aria. 

  

Voice of Magic 

In order to cast an aria efficiently you must be able to speak and move. Physical movements that take the form of dances make casting an aria easier and add to its power. However, you do not need a free hand to cast or maintain concentration on an aria. 

If you are unable to move freely (as when you are restrained) and/or speak properly (as when you are gagged or have lost your tongue or teeth), you cast arias at twice their normal vitality cost. An aria cast in this way does not become silent or subtle. 

The caster is the source of an aria's magic, which is why recordings or transmissions of one have no power 

  

Note: Covering Your Ears 

There are many things that prevent you from hearing well, including stuffing wax in your ears or simply putting on headphones. This does not protect from arias, but obstacles that completely block sound will block an aria. 

  

Verses 

Every aria has at least two verses, with each verse increasing the power of the aria. 

If you maintain concentration until the start of your next turn, regardless of the aria's casting time, you may use your action to empower the aria by starting its next verse. Unless stated otherwise, the effects of higher-level verses stack with those of a lower level. You cannot enter an aria's second verse on the turn during which you first began the aria and you cannot enter into another verse more than once per turn. 

If an aria ends, either because you lose concentration or decide to end it, you must start over from its first verse when you cast it again. 

 

Areas of Effect 

Most arias affect any creature of the casters choice in range. This means that when a creature comes into the arias range while the caster is concentrating on it, the caster may choose to have that creature be affected, even if it was not in range when the aria started. In turn, when a creature gets out of the aria's range, the effect usually ends for it on the first turn it starts outside the aria. You can basically imagine these arias as an invisible dome that is always centered on the caster. Anything inside can be affected, but only for as long as it remains inside that dome. 

  

Environment Based Damage 

Some arias resonate with the environment they are cast in to produce elemental energy. 

Whenever an aria calls for environmental damage, refer to the Environmental Damage Type table to determine the type of damage dealt. 

The type of environment is determined by the landscape surrounding the caster. The terrain underwater is just as varied as above the waves and different areas underwater provide different damage types, just like on land. The GM has final say on the damage type your spell deals.

Novice (8) 

Thursday, June 16, 2022 

8:50 AM 

Novice 

Access to novice arias grants you the abilities described under Channel Performance I and allows you to learn the following spells. 

 

Channel Performance I 

The first step to learning the art of aria singing, is weaving magic into mundane seeming noises. At its simplest this allows you to unleash sound that resonates with the very fabric of the world. 

 

Compounding Crescendo. At a negligible cost to your vitality you may use your action to target a creature no more than 10 meters away. The target must succeed a Constitution saving throw or take 1d6 thunder damage. Succeed or fail, the target then takes 1d6 thunder damage for each crescendo counter applied to it and its crescendo count increases by 1. A creature is cleansed of your crescendo counters when you target another creature with this ability, or if it has received no crescendo counters from you in the last minute. 

(Prototype) Vicious Mockery. At a negligible cost to your vitality you may use your action to target a creature no more than 10 meters away. The target must succeed a Wisdom saving throw or take 1d4 psychic damage and have disadvantage on attack rolls and saving throws until the start of your next turn. 

Center of Attention. As an action you expend a negligible amount of vitality to weave a distracting magical tune until you lose concentration. You are revealed and all creatures of your choice that you can see no more than 10 meters away have disadvantage on Wisdom (Perception) checks made to perceive any creature other than you until the spell ends or an affected creature starts its turn out of range. A creature that is immune to being charmed is immune to this effect. 

Marching Song. For a negligible cost to your vitality, you may inspire your companions and yourself using tunes and rhythms laced with magic. You may travel for 10 hours each day instead of 8 before having to make exhaustion checks. You cannot travel stealthily while using this ability, as your tunes alert everyone nearby to your presence. 

Mark Unnatural. As an action you expend a negligible amount of vitality to begin a quiet tune that lasts until you lose concentration. For the duration, sources of unnatural energy in your vicinity are highlighted by a glow who's color depends on the type of energy.  

This spell highlights magical effects, fires (including engines) and active electronic components within 10 meters of you. Celestials, elementals, fiends, and undead within 10 meters of you are also highlighted and revealed.  

The effect ends for a target if it or its wielder starts its turn out of range or behind total cover. If the target is an item that's being neither worn nor carried, the effect ends if you end your turn while the item is out of range or behind total cover.

Friday, June 24, 2022 

11:02 PM 

Befriending 

Novice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 1 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 30 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

A subtle and plain tune is woven into your speech. Your words resonate not just with the ears of those that hear them, but with their very minds. All understand it and as the melody progresses, it puts its listeners at ease and opens them to your requests. 

 

1st Verse: While you perform this aria, you may speak (sing or rap) phrases. Any creature in range understands the meaning of your performance, even if you do not share a language or it cannot speak. Its ability to act on this understanding may be limited by its Intelligence and Wisdom. A creature with 2 or less Intelligence cannot be communicated with in any meaningful way. 

2nd Verse: You have advantage on deception, intimidation, performance, and persuasion checks directed at creatures in range. 

3rd Verse: At the end of each of your turns, all creature of your choice in range must succeed a Wisdom saving throw or be charmed by you for the duration, or until it takes damage. A creature you are in combat with has advantage on this save. The effect also ends for a creature if it starts its turn out of range or behind total cover.

Thursday, January 13, 2022 

8:44 AM 

Detecting 

Novice aria 

Vitality Cost: 1 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: Self 

Duration: Concentration 

  

As you start this slow melody, it resonates deeply, reverberating through the air and uncovering secrets hidden from your sight. As the aria grows in power, it penetrates even through wood, stone, and steel, laying bare what was once concealed. 

  

1st Verse: You gain 20 meters of blindsight for the duration. Any space or surface that sound cannot pass through is considered opaque to this sense. 

2nd Verse: The range of your blindsight increases by 10 meters to 30 meters.  

3rd Verse: The range of your blindsight again increases by 10 meters to 40 meters. Your blindsight allows you to see through up to 1 meter of solid material.

Empowering 

Novice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 2 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 10 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You start a fast and lively tune that focuses the minds of your allies. 

 

For the duration, you may affect any creature of your choice in range until it starts its turn out of range. 

1st Verse: Whenever an affected creatures makes an attack roll or a saving throw before the spell ends, it may roll a d4 and add the number rolled to the attack roll or saving throw. 

2nd Verse: Instead of a d4, targets may roll a d6. 

3rd Verse: Instead of a d6, targets may roll a d8.

Friday, June 17, 2022 

9:13 AM 

Hampering 

Novice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 2 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: Self (10-meter radius) 

Duration: Concentration 

 

A wavering tune causes your foes to become disoriented. The effect is subtle at first, but as the intensity of your harmony increases, it becomes crippling. 

 

When a creature would be affected by this spell, you may choose to not affect it instead (no action required). 

1st Verse: The ground and air in a 10-meter radius sphere centered on you becomes difficult terrain. 

2nd Verse: Creatures no more than 10 meters away have disadvantage on attack rolls. 

3rd Verse: A creature that starts its turn no more than 10 meters away from you, or enters that area during its turn must succeed a Constitution saving throw or be restrained until the start of its next turn.

Thursday, January 13, 2022 

8:44 AM 

Mesmerizing 

Novice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 1 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 10 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You begin a thin, high pitched tune that charms one who can hear it. As the music picks up, they become more and more focused on you until slowly slouching towards you is all they can do. 

 

1st Verse: At the end of each of your turns, every creature of your choice in range must succeed a Wisdom saving throw or be charmed by you. A charmed creature may repeat the saving throw whenever it takes damage from a source other than you. A creature also repeats the saving throw at the end of each of its turns. If a creature's saving throw is successful, the effect ends for it and it can no longer be affected by this casting of the aria. The effect also ends for a creature if the affected creature starts its turn out of range or behind total cover. 

2nd Verse: While the target is charmed by you, it is incapacitated. 

3rd Verse: If the charmed target is more than 1 meter away from you, if it can the target must move on its turn toward you by the most direct route. It doesn't avoid opportunity attacks, but before moving into damaging terrain, such as off a cliff, and whenever it takes damage from a source other than you, a target can repeat the saving throw.

Saturday, June 18, 2022 

2:30 PM 

Vitalizing 

Novice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 4 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 10 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

A harmonious tune frees the flow of energy through the veil around a friend. The strain of casting decreases and as the melody progresses, the intensity of their spells increases. 

 

For the duration, you may affect any creature of your choice in range until it starts its turn out of range. 

1st Verse: At the end of each of your turns, all affected creatures gain 1d4 temporary vitality for the duration of the aria. This temporary vitality does not stack. 

2nd Verse: The spellcasting modifiers of affected creatures increase by 2, increasing the Save DC of their spells and their modifiers for Spell Attacks accordingly. 

3rd Verse: Whenever an affected creature cast a spell, it may upcast that spell once without expending additional vitality.

Apprentice (6) 

Thursday, June 16, 2022 

8:50 AM 

Apprentice 

Access to apprentice arias grants you the ability described under Channel Performance II and allows you to learn the following spells. 

 

Channel Performance II 

As you increase in skill, you gain the ability to do more than subtly influence others bodies and spirits through song. At is simplest this allows you to amplify your voice, increasing the range of your arias and granting you the ability to unleash devastating sonic blasts that only affect your foes. 

 

Amplified Performance. You may increase the volume of your voice and any instruments you are playing by up to three times. 

The range of your Compounding Crescendo, Center of Attention and Mark Unnatural abilities (see Channel Performance I) increases to 30 meters (from 10).

Thursday, January 13, 2022 

8:44 AM 

Castigating 

Apprentice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 2 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: Self 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You start a subtle, thick melody. Whenever a foe comes into contact with your body, a burst of thunderous vengeance retaliates.  

 

1st Verse: For the duration, whenever a creature touches or hits you with a melee attack it makes a Dexterity saving throw. It takes 2d6 thunder damage on a failed save, or half as much on a successful one. 

2nd Verse: The damage a creature takes on a failed save increases by 2d6 (to 4d6) thunder damage. 

3rd Verse: The damage a creature takes on a failed save increases by 4d6 (to 8d6) thunder damage.

Thursday, January 13, 2022 

8:44 AM 

Revealing 

Apprentice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 3 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 30 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

A lively tune springs forth, causing all that is hidden to be revealed by flakes of glittering light. As the melody reaches its apex, the intensity of the spell makes defending against attacks much harder. 

 

1st Verse: Every creature and invisible object or effect of your choice in range is revealed by an aura of glittering light for the duration, or until it is no longer in range. 

2nd Verse: Attacks against creatures revealed by this spell have advantage.

Shielding 

Apprentice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 1 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 10 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You begin a steady melody that emanates out from you. Around your allies, the air begins to quiver from your harmonics, leaving them unimpeded, but disturbing the path of all that would harm them. As your tune increases in intensity, so does the protection it provides.  

 

 

For the duration, you may affect any creature of your choice in range until it starts its turn out of range. 

1st Verse: Affected creatures have their AC increased by 1. 

2nd Verse: The bonus AC increases to 2. 

3rd Verse: The bonus AC increases to 3.

Saturday, June 18, 2022 

10:08 AM 

Striding 

Apprentice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 2 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 30 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You begin a fast and simple tune that lifts the bodies and spirits of your allies. Each verse you sing increases their agility and speed. 

 

For the duration, you may affect any creature of your choice in range until it starts its turn out of range. 

1st Verse: Affected creatures ignore difficult terrain. 

2nd Verse: Affected creatures do not provoke attacks of opportunity. 

3rd Verse: Affected creatures have their speed increased by 5 meters.

8:44 AM 

Stunning 

Apprentice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 2 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 30 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You begin a deep, steady tune and force the insides of a foe to vibrate with the tune. They feel as though they are being crushed and torn apart at the same time from every direction. As you move towards the crescendo, the trauma of your song strikes all but the toughest dumb. 

 

When you start this aria, target a creature in range for the duration or until it starts its turn out of range or behind total cover. You may switch targets as a bonus action.  

1st Verse: At the end of each of your the target makes a Constitution saving throw, taking 2d6 thunder damage on a failed save or half as much on a successful one. 

2nd Verse: A target that fails its saving throw is also knocked prone. 

3rd Verse: A target that fails its saving throw is also stunned until the start of its next turn.

Thursday, January 13, 2022 

8:44 AM 

Thundering 

Apprentice aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 2 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 10 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You begin a deep tune that rises and falls in volume. With every apex a thunderous boom explodes from your body. At first they only burst ear drums, but quickly grow in power to throw those caught through the air like ragdolls. 

 

1st Verse: At the end of each of your turns, each creature of your choice in range takes 1d6 thunder damage. 

2nd Verse: The damage increases by 1d6 (to 2d6) and medium or smaller creatures are pushed 2 meters away from you. 

3rd Verse: The damage increases by 2d6 (to 4d6), the distance a medium or smaller creature it is pushed increases by 4 meters (to 6) and creatures pushed by this spell are knocked prone.

Adept (5) 

Thursday, June 16, 2022 

8:50 AM 

Adept 

Access to adept arias grants you the ability described under Channel Performance III and allows you to learn the following spells. 

 

Channel Performance III 

You gain the ability to amplify the elemental energy flowing through the world and your ability to interact with manifestations of magic expands. At its simplest this makes it possible to summon spikes of magical energy in enchantments to destroy them. 

 

Disenchanting Performance. Spend a 1 minute ritual singing to a magic item no more than 1 meter away. When you finish casting, the magic item crumbles to dust. If it is a glyph, the glyph crumbles out of the object, but leaves it otherwise intact. 

When you use this ability on a construct, beginning the ritual is considered dealing damage to the construct.

Sunday, June 19, 2022 

1:24 PM 

Disenchanting 

Adept aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 3 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 30 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You begin an intense and high pitched tune directed at a magic item or arcane effect. The magical pathways inside it are disrupted and degrade. As the aria progresses, the item begins exuding magical energy, before finally disintegrating. 

 

When you start this aria, target a magic item or construct in range for the duration. The aria ends if the target or its wielder starts its turn out of range or behind total cover. If the target is an item that's being neither worn nor carried, the aria ends if you end your turn while the item is out of range or behind total cover. 

1st Verse: If the target is an item, any traits that require vitality to use have their vitality cost doubled. 

If the target is a construct, double its power consumption. 

2nd Verse: At the end of each of your turns, any creature in physical contact with the object takes 2d8 damage. The damage type dealt to it depends on the item. If the item has an effect that deals acid, cold, fire, force, or lightning damage, then the damage dealt by this spell shares that type. Otherwise it deals fire damage. 

If a creature is holding or wearing the object and takes damage from it, the creature must succeed on a Constitution saving throw or drop the object if it can. If it doesn't drop the object, it has disadvantage on attack rolls and ability checks until the start of your next turn. 

If the target is a construct, it takes 16 armor piercing fire damage. 

3rd Verse: At the end of your turn the item disintegrates and the aria ends. 

If the target is a construct, reduce its current hit points by 100 at the end of each of your turns. If this reduces the target to 0 hit points, it disintegrates and the aria ends.

Thursday, January 13, 2022 

8:44 AM 

Hypnotizing 

Adept aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 4 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 30 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You begin a powerful, yet calming melody. Its effects are slow, but as the magic takes hold, the heads of those affected turn to you and your harrowing tune. Their minds slip away and unless trauma snaps them out of this stupor, they can do nothing but stand and stare vacantly in your direction. 

 

1st Verse: At the end of each of your turns, every creature of your choice in range must succeed a Wisdom saving throw or be charmed by you for the duration. The effect ends for an affected creature if it takes damage, or if someone else uses an action to shake the creature out of its stupor. The effect also ends for a creature if the affected creature starts its turn out of range or behind total cover. 

2nd Verse: While charmed by this aria, an affected creature has disadvantage on attack rolls. 

3rd Verse: While charmed by this aria, an affected creature is incapacitated and has a speed of 0.

Thursday, June 16, 2022 

11:35 AM 

Incandescent 

Adept aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 4 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 20 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You form a crescendo punctuated with spikes in frequency. Energy flows from the beyond, focused through the environment it is coaxed from. Every time an apex is struck, your foes flare with this elemental power.  

 

The type of damage this spell deals depends on the environment you are in (see the Environment Damage Type table). 

1st Verse: At the end of each of your turns, you deal 2d6 damage to every creature of your choice in range.  

2nd Verse: The range of this spell increases by 5 meters to 15 meters. Increase the damage you deal to every creature of your choice in range by 2d6 (to 4d6). 

3rd Verse: The range of this spell increases by 5 meters again to 20 meters. Increase the damage you deal to every creature of your choice in range by 4d6 (to 8d6).

Expert (4) 

Thursday, June 16, 2022 

8:53 AM 

Expert 

Access to expert arias grants you the ability described under Channel Performance IV and allows you to learn the following spells. 

 

Channel Performance IV 

The power and more importantly sophistication with which you can influence others increase. With relative ease you can cause your arias to disrupt other-worldly influences. 

 

Countercharm. As a bonus action you may expend 2 vitality and unleash a spike of music hardens the minds of your allies. Any effect affecting any creature of your choice no more than 30 meters away that would end, if it were immune to being charmed or frightened, ends. 

Conflicting change.

Thursday, January 13, 2022 

8:44 AM 

Destroying 

Expert aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 8 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range:  10 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

You draw incredible power and through a grand melody you unleash a devastating burst of sound and energy. With every note struck, the destruction grows. Everything around you is blasted away and torn apart by the thundering booms and elemental flares. 

 

1st Verse: At the end of each of your turns, you deal 2d6 thunder damage, as well as 2d6 elemental damage to every creature of your choice in range, and medium or smaller creatures are knocked prone. The type of this elemental damage depends on the environment you are in (see the Environment Damage Type table). 

2nd Verse: The range of this spell increases by 5 meters to 15 meters. Increase the damage you then deal to every creature of your choice in range on a failed save by 2d6 (to 4d6) thunder damage and the environment based damage by 2d6 (to 4d6). 

3rd Verse: The range of this spell increases again by 5 meters to 20 meters. Increase the damage you then deal to every creature of your choice in range on a failed save by 2d6 (to 6d6) thunder damage and the environment based damage by 2d6 (to 6d6).

Thursday, January 13, 2022 

8:44 AM 

False  

Expert aria 

 

Vitality Cost: 3 per Turn 

Casting Time: 1 Action 

Range: 30 Meters 

Duration: Concentration 

 

A haunting melody fills the air with a distinct rhythm. It hazes recent memories and makes it hard to focus on you. As the melody becomes clearer, you and your allies slip from the minds of the affected. All that remains is a haunting tune in their minds, the source of which they do not know. 

 

For the duration, you may affect any creature of your choice in range. This aria does not reveal you. 

1st Verse: You gain a +10 bonus to Dexterity (Stealth) checks made to hide from an affected creature. 

2nd Verse: When you become hidden to an affected creature, it loses all awareness and memories of your presence and actions no older than 1 hour.  

3rd Verse: When an affected creature loses sight of you, you become hidden to it (no check required).

Thursday, January 13, 2022
